Collect & Analyze Finance Data from LSEG with Python¶

Alexandre Garel, Finance Professor, Audencia, 2025–2026

1. Global Configuration¶

Before running any of the examples below, run the next cell once. It performs three housekeeping tasks that every subsequent cell depends on.

  1. BASE_FOLDER — We define a single folder path where all CSV files produced in this notebook will be saved. You should change this path to a folder that exists on your own computer.
  2. Pandas display options — By default, pandas (the main data-manipulation library we use) truncates wide tables. We tell it to show all columns and up to 60 rows so that our outputs are easier to read.
  3. Warning suppression — Some libraries print non-critical warning messages. We silence them to keep the notebook output clean. If you ever need to debug an issue, you can comment out the warnings.filterwarnings("ignore") line.
In [3]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import pandas as pd  # pandas is the standard Python library for working with tabular data (DataFrames)
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)
# ── 1. Global configuration (run this cell once) ──────────────────────────────

# 'warnings' lets us control Python warning messages.
import warnings  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell

# 'pandas' (imported as 'pd') is the main library for tabular data in Python.
# A DataFrame — the core pandas object — is like an Excel spreadsheet in code.
import pandas as pd  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell

# Set the folder where every CSV produced in this notebook will be saved.
# >>> Change this path to a folder that exists on YOUR computer. <<<
BASE_FOLDER = r"C:\Users\alexa\Documents\Research methods 2"  # variable assignment: store a value in 'BASE_FOLDER' so we can reuse it later

# Suppress non-critical warnings so the notebook output stays readable.
# Remove or comment out this line when debugging.
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore") 

# Tell pandas to display ALL columns (not truncate wide tables)
# and to show at most 60 rows before summarising.
pd.set_option("display.max_columns", None) 
pd.set_option("display.max_rows", 60)

Running code. Each gray box below is a code cell. Click inside it and press Shift+Enter (or click Run) to execute the code. The result appears under the cell. Run cells from top to bottom when the text says so.

Variables. You store values in variables with =. Once a variable exists, you can use it in later lines or cells.

Example:

In [1]:
# Variables and simple operations
x = 10
y = 3
print("x =", x, ", y =", y)
print("x + y =", x + y)
print("x * y =", x * y)

x = 10 , y = 3
x + y = 13
x * y = 30

NumPy: turning values into numbers and dropping missing values¶

What is NumPy? NumPy is a library for numerical data. It gives you arrays: lists of numbers (or other types) that you can compute with.

Turning text into numbers. If your data is text (e.g. "3.14"), you need to convert it to numbers. In pandas we use pd.to_numeric(..., errors="coerce"): it turns valid text into numbers and invalid text (e.g. "missing") into NaN (missing). We use a pd.Series() so we can then use .dropna().

Dropping missing values. In NumPy you can use np.isnan() to find NaNs and then keep only the valid entries. In pandas we use .dropna() on a Series or DataFrame to remove rows with missing values. The example below uses pandas for both steps (convert to numeric, then drop missing).

In [14]:
import pandas as pd

# List of strings (some are numbers, one is not)
strings = ["1.5", "2.7", "missing", "4.0"]
print("Strings:", strings)

# Turn to numeric: invalid text becomes NaN
numeric = pd.to_numeric(pd.Series(strings), errors="coerce")
print("Numeric (with NaN):", numeric.tolist())

# Drop missing values
clean = numeric.dropna()
print("After dropna():", clean.tolist())

Strings: ['1.5', '2.7', 'missing', '4.0']
Numeric (with NaN): [1.5, 2.7, nan, 4.0]
After dropna(): [1.5, 2.7, 4.0]

pandas: Series and DataFrames¶

What is a Series? A Series is a single column of data with a name and an index (row labels). You can do math, filter, and use .dropna() on it.

What is a DataFrame? A DataFrame is a table: several columns (each a Series) sharing the same index. Rows = observations, columns = variables. Most datasets (CSV, Excel, etc.) are loaded as DataFrames.

How to handle them:

  • Create: pd.DataFrame({"col1": [1,2,3], "col2": ["a","b","c"]})
  • Columns: df["col_name"] or df.col_name gives a Series.
  • Rows: df.loc[0] (by label), df.iloc[0] (by position).
  • Rename columns: df.rename(columns={"old_name": "new_name"})
  • Subset rows: df[df["col"] > 0]
In [15]:
import pandas as pd

# Create a DataFrame
df = pd.DataFrame({"score": [85, 90, 78], "name": ["Alice", "Bob", "Charlie"]})
print("DataFrame:")
print(df)

# Columns: each column is a Series
print("\nColumn 'score' (Series):")
print(df["score"])

# Rows: by label (loc) or position (iloc)
print("\nFirst row (loc):", df.loc[0].tolist())
print("First row (iloc):", df.iloc[0].tolist())

# Rename columns
df_renamed = df.rename(columns={"score": "grade", "name": "student"})
print("\nAfter rename:")
print(df_renamed)

# Subset: keep only rows where grade > 82
print("\nRows where grade > 82:")
print(df_renamed[df_renamed["grade"] > 82])

DataFrame:
   score     name
0     85    Alice
1     90      Bob
2     78  Charlie

Column 'score' (Series):
0    85
1    90
2    78
Name: score, dtype: int64

First row (loc): [85, 'Alice']
First row (iloc): [85, 'Alice']

After rename:
   grade  student
0     85    Alice
1     90      Bob
2     78  Charlie

Rows where grade > 82:
   grade student
0     85   Alice
1     90     Bob

Other core DataFrame functions¶

Inspecting data

  • df.head(n) — first n rows (default 5). Use df.tail(n) for the last n rows.
  • df.shape — number of rows and columns, e.g. (100, 5).
  • df.columns — list of column names.
  • df.dtypes — type of each column (int, float, object, etc.).

Combining tables: merge

  • pd.merge(left_df, right_df, on="col") — join two DataFrames on a common column (like SQL JOIN). Use how="left", "right", "outer", "inner" to choose the type of join.

Other useful operations

  • df.dropna() — drop rows with missing values (NaN).
  • df.fillna(value) — replace missing values with a value.
  • df.sort_values("col") — sort rows by a column (use ascending=False for descending).
  • df.describe() — summary statistics (count, mean, std, min, max, etc.) for numeric columns.
In [16]:
import pandas as pd

# Two small tables
grades = pd.DataFrame({"student_id": [1, 2, 3], "grade": [85, 90, 78]})
names  = pd.DataFrame({"student_id": [1, 2, 3], "name": ["Alice", "Bob", "Charlie"]})

print("Table 1 (grades):")
print(grades)
print("\nTable 2 (names):")
print(names)

# head / tail / shape
print("\nFirst 2 rows (head):")
print(grades.head(2))
print("Shape (rows, cols):", grades.shape)
print("Column names:", grades.columns.tolist())

# merge: join on student_id
merged = pd.merge(grades, names, on="student_id")
print("\nMerged (join on 'student_id'):")
print(merged)

# sort and describe
print("\nSorted by grade (descending):")
print(merged.sort_values("grade", ascending=False))
print("\nSummary stats (describe):")
print(merged.describe())

Table 1 (grades):
   student_id  grade
0           1     85
1           2     90
2           3     78

Table 2 (names):
   student_id     name
0           1    Alice
1           2      Bob
2           3  Charlie

First 2 rows (head):
   student_id  grade
0           1     85
1           2     90
Shape (rows, cols): (3, 2)
Column names: ['student_id', 'grade']

Merged (join on 'student_id'):
   student_id  grade     name
0           1     85    Alice
1           2     90      Bob
2           3     78  Charlie

Sorted by grade (descending):
   student_id  grade     name
1           2     90      Bob
0           1     85    Alice
2           3     78  Charlie

Summary stats (describe):
       student_id      grade
count         3.0   3.000000
mean          2.0  84.333333
std           1.0   6.027714
min           1.0  78.000000
25%           1.5  81.500000
50%           2.0  85.000000
75%           2.5  87.500000
max           3.0  90.000000

2. Collecting Data from Refinitiv / LSEG with Python¶

In this section we learn how to connect to the Refinitiv / LSEG financial data platform and download market, accounting, ESG, and news data directly into Python. Every subsection follows the same pattern: open a connection, request specific data by specifying an instrument and a set of fields, inspect the result, save it as a CSV file, and close the connection.

2.1. What is a Python package?¶

A Python package is a collection of pre-written code that provides specific functionality. Instead of writing everything from scratch, we install a package and call the functions it provides.

Packages are installed with the pip tool. When running pip from inside a Jupyter Notebook cell, prefix the command with ! (for example !pip install refinitiv-data).

refinitiv-data is the official Python SDK (Software Development Kit) provided by Refinitiv (now LSEG). It lets us programmatically access:

  • Market data (prices, time series, intraday data).
  • Reference data (instruments, identifiers, metadata).
  • Fundamental and economic data (accounting, ratios).
  • News and analytics.

The package handles authentication, data requests, and responses behind the scenes. The cell below installs it into the current Anaconda environment. If it is already installed, pip will simply report that the requirement is satisfied.

In [14]:
!pip install refinitiv-data

2.2. Reading official documentation¶

Once a package is installed, the next step is to read its official documentation. Official documentation is the most reliable source because it describes the intended usage, explains inputs and outputs, provides examples, and reflects the most up-to-date behaviour of the package.

Key documentation sources for the Refinitiv Data Library:

  • GitHub repository: https://github.com/refinitiv/refinitiv-data
  • Developer portal: https://developers.refinitiv.com/

Alternatively, you can ask an AI assistant (Claude is recommended) to help generate code. AI can draft code snippets, explain unfamiliar functions, help debug errors, and suggest efficient approaches. However, AI-generated code must always be reviewed to ensure you understand what it does, can validate the outputs, and can catch any outdated or incorrect API usage.

A robust approach is to constrain the AI by providing the official documentation as input and asking it to answer only based on that material, or by asking it to consult the official online documentation first.

Two practical tools inside Python for self-documentation are:

  • help() — Displays the signature and parameters of a function. Example: help(rd.get_data).
  • search.metadata.Definition(view=...) — Returns a table of all properties available for a given Search view (for example EQUITY_QUOTES, GOV_CORP_INSTRUMENTS), indicating which ones are searchable, groupable, and filterable.

Whenever you design a new query, it is good practice to first read the relevant section of the LSEG documentation and then inspect the metadata and help() output in Python so that your filters and field lists are based on what the platform actually supports.

2.3. Exploring available functions and fields¶

Before writing a data query, you may want to know what functions are available in the rd module and how to use them. Python provides two built-in helpers for this.

dir() — Lists every function and attribute available in a module. Running dir(rd) prints the names of everything you can call on the rd object.

help() — Shows the full documentation of a specific function, including its parameters, their types, default values, and a short description. For example, help(rd.get_history) explains what arguments get_history accepts.

The cell below demonstrates both.

In [15]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd 
# import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell
# List all available functions and attributes in the rd module.
# You will see names like 'get_history', 'get_data', 'discovery', 'news', etc.
print(dir(rd))  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

['OpenState', 'PricingStream', 'close_session', 'content', 'dates_and_calendars', 'delivery', 'discovery', 'eikon', 'errors', 'get_config', 'get_data', 'get_history', 'load_config', 'news', 'open_pricing_stream', 'open_session', 'session', 'usage_collection']

Here we have used the command print, print() sends information from your program to the standard output, usually the screen (console/terminal). It is important because it’s the simplest way to see what your code is doing. It connects the invisible internal world of the program (variables, calculations, logic) with what you can actually see and understand as a human.

In [16]:
#illustration 
name = "Alex"  # variable assignment: store a value in 'name' so we can reuse it later
age = 21  # variable assignment: store a value in 'age' so we can reuse it later
print("Name:", name)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print("Age:", age)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

Name: Alex
Age: 21
In [7]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
try:  # try/except lets us handle the case where credentials are missing
    rd.open_session()  # open a session so we can request data from the platform
except Exception as e:  # if something goes wrong, we catch the error instead of crashing
    print('Could not open a Refinitiv/LSEG session:', e)  # explain the issue to the student
# illustration of how to use help
# Import the Refinitiv Data Library and assign it the short alias 'rd'.
import refinitiv.data as rd  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell
# Get help
help(rd.get_data) 

Help on function get_data in module refinitiv.data._access_layer.get_data_func:

get_data(universe: Union[str, Iterable[str]], fields: Union[str, Iterable[str], NoneType] = None, parameters: Union[str, dict, NoneType] = None, use_field_names_in_headers: bool = None) -> pandas.core.frame.DataFrame
    Retrieves pricing snapshots, as well as Fundamental and Reference data.

    Parameters
    ----------
    universe: str | list
        Instruments to request
    fields: str | list, optional
        Fields to request
    parameters: str | dict, optional
        Single key=value global parameter or dictionary of global parameters to request
    use_field_names_in_headers: bool, default False
        If True - returns field name as column headers for data instead of title

    Returns
    -------
    pandas.DataFrame

    Examples
    --------
    >>> get_data(universe=['IBM.N', 'VOD.L'], fields=['BID', 'ASK'])
    >>> get_data(
    ...     universe=['GOOG.O', 'AAPL.O'],
    ...     fields=['TR.EV','TR.EVToSales'],
    ...     parameters = {'SDate': '0CY', 'Curn': 'CAD'}
    ...)
In [8]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
try:  # try/except lets us handle the case where credentials are missing
    rd.open_session()  # open a session so we can request data from the platform
except Exception as e:  # if something goes wrong, we catch the error instead of crashing
    print('Could not open a Refinitiv/LSEG session:', e)  # explain the issue to the student
# illustration of how to use help
# Import the Refinitiv Data Library and assign it the short alias 'rd'.
import refinitiv.data as rd  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell
# Get help
help(rd.get_history) 

Help on function get_history in module refinitiv.data._access_layer.get_history_func:

get_history(universe: Union[str, Iterable[str]], fields: Union[str, Iterable[str], NoneType] = None, interval: Optional[str] = None, start: 'OptDateTime' = None, end: 'OptDateTime' = None, adjustments: Optional[str] = None, count: Optional[int] = None, use_field_names_in_headers: bool = None, parameters: Union[str, dict, NoneType] = None) -> pandas.core.frame.DataFrame
    Retrieves the pricing history, as well as Fundamental and Reference data history.

    Parameters
    ----------
    universe: str | list
        Instruments to request
    fields: str | list, optional
        Fields to request
    interval: str, optional
        Date interval. Supported intervals are:
        tick, tas, taq, minute, 1min, 5min, 10min, 30min, 60min, hourly, 1h, daily,
        1d, 1D, 7D, 7d, weekly, 1W, monthly, 1M, quarterly, 3M, 6M, yearly, 1Y
    start: str or date or datetime or timedelta, optional
        The start date and timestamp of the requested history
    end: str or date or datetime or timedelta, optional
        The end date and timestamp of the requested history
    adjustments : str, optional
        Tells the system whether to apply or not apply CORAX (Corporate Actions)
        events or exchange/manual corrections or price and volume adjustment
        according to trade/quote qualifier summarization actions to historical time
        series data. Possible values are:
        exchangeCorrection, manualCorrection, CCH, CRE, RTS, RPO, unadjusted,
        qualifiers
    count : int, optional
        The maximum number of data points returned. Values range: 1 - 10000.
        Applies only to pricing fields.
    use_field_names_in_headers : bool, default False
        If True - returns field name as column headers for data instead of title
    parameters: str | dict, optional
        Single global parameter key=value or dictionary
        of global parameters to request.
        Applies only to TR fields.

    Returns
    -------
    pandas.DataFrame

    Examples
    --------
    >>> get_history(universe="GOOG.O")
    >>> get_history(universe="GOOG.O", fields="tr.Revenue", interval="1Y")
    >>> get_history(
    ...     universe="GOOG.O",
    ...     fields=["BID", "ASK", "tr.Revenue"],
    ...     interval="1Y",
    ...     start="2015-01-01",
    ...     end="2020-10-01",
    ... )

2.4. A first data extraction¶

In this subsection we run a first simple extraction to illustrate the basic workflow.

Step-by-step logic:

  1. Import the package and give it the short alias rd so we can write rd.get_history() instead of refinitiv.data.get_history().
  2. Open a session with rd.open_session(). This authenticates you and establishes a connection to Refinitiv services. It automatically detects a running Refinitiv Workspace application.
  3. Request data with rd.get_history(). We provide:
    • universe — the instrument we want data for, identified by its RIC (Reuters Instrument Code). For example, "AAPL.O" is Apple Inc. on NASDAQ.
    • fields — the specific data points to retrieve, here "BID" (the price a buyer is willing to pay) and "ASK" (the price a seller is willing to accept).
    • start / end — the date range for the historical data.
  4. Save the result as a CSV file so we can reuse it later without re-downloading.
  5. Close the session with rd.close_session() to release the connection.

The function returns a DataFrame — the core pandas object, which you can think of as a spreadsheet stored in Python's memory.

In [9]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import pandas as pd  # pandas is the standard Python library for working with tabular data (DataFrames)
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
from datetime import datetime, date  # datetime provides date/time objects and parsing
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
try:  # try/except lets us handle the case where credentials are missing
    rd.open_session()  # open a session so we can request data from the platform
except Exception as e:  # if something goes wrong, we catch the error instead of crashing
    print('Could not open a Refinitiv/LSEG session:', e)  # explain the issue to the student
# Import the Refinitiv Data Library and assign it the short alias 'rd'.
import refinitiv.data as rd  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell

# Open a session (auto-detects a running Refinitiv Workspace application).
rd.open_session()  # rd.open_session() starts an authenticated connection to Refinitiv/LSEG (needed before queries)

# Request historical BID and ASK prices for Apple (AAPL.O) over 2024–2025.
# 'universe' = the instrument; 'fields' = the data points; 'start'/'end' = date range.
df = rd.get_history(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'df' so we can reuse it later
    universe="AAPL.O",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=["BID", "ASK"],  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
    start="2024-01-01",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'start' for the function call
    end="2025-12-31"  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'end' for the function call
) 

# Print the first rows of the resulting DataFrame.
print(df)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# Save the data as a CSV file in the base folder defined in Section 1.
output_path = BASE_FOLDER + "\\aapl.csv"  # variable assignment: store a value in 'output_path' so we can reuse it later
df.to_csv(output_path)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk

# Close the session to release the connection.
rd.close_session() 

AAPL.O         BID     ASK
Date                      
2024-01-02  185.52  185.53
2024-01-03  184.24  184.25
2024-01-04  181.91  181.92
2024-01-05  181.17  181.19
2024-01-08  185.54  185.55
...            ...     ...
2025-12-24  273.82  273.88
2025-12-26  273.25  273.45
2025-12-29  273.74  273.76
2025-12-30  272.98  273.09
2025-12-31  271.84  271.94

[502 rows x 2 columns]

What fields can I retrieve for a given instrument?

For a complete catalogue of all available data items and their exact code names (called TR mnemonics), use two desktop tools inside Refinitiv Workspace:

  • Data Item Browser (DIB) — type DIB in the Workspace search bar. It lets you browse all data items and see their exact mnemonics.
  • CodeCreator (CODECR) — type CODECR in the Workspace search bar. It lets you select data items and automatically generates example Python code that you can copy-paste directly into your notebook.

Application. Now it is your turn to practice. Find the RIC for Microsoft, read the help for get_history() to understand the parameters it can take and look at the variables we can retrieve with it. You must retrieve for Microsoft the monthly time series of volume over 2023. Try to do it yourself. A solution is provided below (click to unhide it).

In [17]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)

# Open a session.
rd.open_session()  # rd.open_session() starts an authenticated connection to Refinitiv/LSEG (needed before queries)

# traded volume: ACVOL_UNS
df = rd.get_history(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'df' so we can reuse it later
universe="MSFT.O",  # variable assignment: store a value in 'universe' so we can reuse it later
fields=["ACVOL_UNS"],  # variable assignment: store a value in 'fields' so we can reuse it later
interval="1M",  # variable assignment: store a value in 'interval' so we can reuse it later
start="2023-01-01",  # variable assignment: store a value in 'start' so we can reuse it later
end="2023-12-31")  # variable assignment: store a value in 'end' so we can reuse it later

# show dataframe
print(df)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# Close the session.
rd.close_session() 

MSFT.O      ACVOL_UNS
Date                 
2023-01-31  666168068
2023-02-28  615540778
2023-03-31  747751976
2023-04-30  551520127
2023-05-31  600856941
2023-06-30  547748450
2023-07-31  666956355
2023-08-31  479669427
2023-09-30  416768824
2023-10-31  540963749
2023-11-30  564165364
2023-12-31  522081429

2.5. Finding the right RIC identifier and collecting returns¶

Before downloading data for a specific stock or index, you need its RIC (Reuters Instrument Code) — a short alphanumeric identifier that uniquely identifies an instrument on Refinitiv. For example, "AAPL.O" is Apple on NASDAQ and ".FCHI" is the CAC 40 price index.

You can look up a RIC in two ways:

  1. Programmatically — Use rd.discovery.search(), which queries the Refinitiv search engine. You provide a free-text query (e.g. "Amazon"), a view (e.g. EQUITY_QUOTES for stocks, INDEX_QUOTES for indices), and a top parameter that limits the number of results.
  2. Interactively — Type the company name directly in the Refinitiv Workspace search bar and note the RIC shown next to the correct instrument.

The cell below shows the help() output for rd.discovery.search so you can see all its parameters, then demonstrates a search for Amazon.

In [18]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
rd.open_session()

# Show the full documentation for the discovery.search function.
help(rd.discovery.search) 

Help on function search in module refinitiv.data.discovery._search:

search(query: Optional[str] = None, view: Union[refinitiv.data.content.search._views.Views, str] = <Views.SEARCH_ALL: 'SearchAll'>, filter: Optional[str] = None, order_by: Optional[str] = None, boost: Optional[str] = None, select: Optional[str] = None, top: int = 10, skip: int = 0, group_by: Optional[str] = None, group_count: int = 3, features: Optional[str] = None, scope: Optional[str] = None, terms: Optional[str] = None) -> 'pd.DataFrame'
    This class describe parameters to retrieve data for search.

    Parameters
    ----------
    query: str, optional
        Keyword argument for view

    view: Views or str, optional
        The view for searching see at Views enum.
        Default: Views.SEARCH_ALL

    filter: str, optional
        Where query is for unstructured end-user-oriented restriction, filter is for
        structured programmatic restriction.

    order_by: str, optional
        Defines the order in which matching documents should be returned.

    boost: str, optional
        This argument supports exactly the same predicate expression syntax as filter,
        but where filter restricts which documents are matched at all,
        boost just applies a large scoring boost to documents it matches,
        which will almost always guarantee that they appear at the top of the results.

    select: str, optional
        A comma-separated list of the properties of a document to be returned in the response.

    top: int, optional
        the maximum number of documents to retrieve. Must be non-negative.
        default: 10

    skip: int, optional
        The number of documents to skip in the sorted result set before returning the
        next top.

    group_by: str, optional
        If specified, this must name a single Groupable property.
        returned documents are grouped into buckets based on their value for this
        property.

    group_count: str, optional
        When supplied in combination with group_by, sets the maximum number of documents
        to be returned per bucket.
        default: 3

    Examples
    --------
    >>> from refinitiv.data as rd
    >>> df = rd.discovery.search(query="cfo", view=rd.discovery.Views.PEOPLE)

The cell below lists all available Search views. Each view gives access to a different category of instrument or dataset (equities, indices, bonds, deals, investors, etc.).

In [19]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
rd.open_session()
 
# List all available Search views (e.g. EQUITY_QUOTES, INDEX_QUOTES, etc.).
# Each view gives access to a different type of instrument or dataset.
views = [v for v in dir(rd.discovery.Views) if not v.startswith("__")]  # variable assignment: store a value in 'views' so we can reuse it later
for i, v in enumerate(views, start=1):  # for-loop: repeat code for each element in an iterable
    print(f"{i:2d}. {v}")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

The cell below searches for Amazon within the EQUITY_QUOTES view and returns the top 10 matches. Inspect the RIC column to find the correct identifier.

In [30]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
rd.open_session()

# Search for "Amazon" in the EQUITY_QUOTES view (stocks only).
# 'top=10' limits the results to the 10 best matches.
results = rd.discovery.search(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'results' so we can reuse it later
    query="Amazon",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'query' for the function call
    view=rd.discovery.Views.EQUITY_QUOTES,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'view' for the function call
    top=10  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'top' for the function call
) 
print(results)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# Save results so we can inspect them offline.
results.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\search_amazon_equity.csv", index=True)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk

rd.close_session()

3   QUOTExEQUITY  Intercontinental Exchange Amazon Flex Equity F...   
4   QUOTExEQUITY  Johannesburg Stock Exchange Amazon Com Interna...   
5   QUOTExEQUITY  Euronext Amsterdam Amazon.com Dividend Future ...   
6   QUOTExEQUITY  Johannesburg Stock Exchange Amazon Com IDX Equ...   
7   QUOTExEQUITY  Johannesburg Stock Exchange Amazon Com Interna...   
8   QUOTExEQUITY  Euronext Amsterdam Amazon com Single Stock Div...   
9   QUOTExEQUITY  Johannesburg Stock Exchange Amazon Com IDX Equ...   

        PermID         PI        RIC

2  22090423093  876385463    0#AMZX:

6  21481187383   79461287   0#AMZIG:

8  21612960409  259721843   AMZNDFc1  
9  21481187455   79537081    AMZIGc1

Once we have the correct RIC (for example "AMZN.O" for Amazon on NASDAQ), we can retrieve a time series of monthly total returns. A total return includes both the change in the stock price and any dividends paid during the period, which gives a more complete picture of the return to shareholders than price returns alone.

The field TR.TotalReturn1Mo provides the one-month total return. We set interval="monthly" so that we get one observation per month.

In [29]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
rd.open_session()

# Download monthly total returns (including dividends) for Amazon (AMZN.O).
df = rd.get_history(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'df' so we can reuse it later
    universe=["AMZN.O"],  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=["TR.TotalReturn1Mo"],   # one-month total return
    interval="monthly",             # one observation per calendar month
    start="2004-01-01",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'start' for the function call
    end="2024-12-31"  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'end' for the function call
) 
print(df)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

rd.close_session() 

AMZN.O      1 Month Total Return
Date                            
2004-01-30             -4.218928
2004-02-27            -12.616822
2004-03-31              0.627761
2004-04-30             -0.456621
2004-05-28             11.238532
...                          ...
2024-08-30             -4.535244
2024-09-30              4.386555
2024-10-31              0.037568
2024-11-29              7.865926
2024-12-31              5.531772

[252 rows x 1 columns]

Application. Now it is your turn to practice. Find the RIC for TESLA using the approach above and then retrieve daily total return over 2024. A solution is provided below (click to unhide it).

In [28]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
rd.open_session()

# Download daily total returns (including dividends) for TESLA (TSLA.O).
df = rd.get_history(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'df' so we can reuse it later
    universe=["TSLA.O"],  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=["TR.TotalReturn1D"],   # one-day total return
    interval="daily",             # one observation per calendar day
    start="2024-01-01",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'start' for the function call
    end="2024-12-31"  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'end' for the function call
) 
print(df)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

rd.close_session() 

TSLA.O      Daily Total Return
Date                          
2024-01-02           -0.024147
2024-01-03           -4.013364
2024-01-04           -0.218075
2024-01-05           -0.184928
2024-01-08            1.246368
...                        ...
2024-12-24            7.357176
2024-12-26           -1.763001
2024-12-27           -4.947922
2024-12-30           -3.301209

[252 rows x 1 columns]

We now search for the RIC of the CAC 40 index. The CAC 40 is the main French stock market index, composed of the 40 largest companies listed on Euronext Paris. We search in the INDEX_QUOTES view.

In [27]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
rd.open_session()

# Search for "CAC40" in the INDEX_QUOTES view (indices only).
results = rd.discovery.search(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'results' so we can reuse it later
    query="CAC40",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'query' for the function call
    view=rd.discovery.Views.INDEX_QUOTES,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'view' for the function call
    top=10  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'top' for the function call
) 
print(results)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# Save search results to CSV.
results.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\search_cac40_index.csv", index=True)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk

rd.close_session() 

  BusinessEntity                                      DocumentTitle  \
0    QUOTExINDEX         CAC 40 Index, Equity Index, Euronext Paris   
1    QUOTExINDEX  CAC 40 Index, Equity Index, Chain, Euronext Paris   
2    QUOTExINDEX  CAC 40 Gross Total Return Index, Equity Index,...   
3    QUOTExINDEX  CAC 40 Net Total Return, Equity Index, Euronex...   
4    QUOTExINDEX     CAC 40 ESG Index, Equity Index, Euronext Paris   
5    QUOTExINDEX  CAC 40 Equal Weight GR Index, Equity Index, Eu...   
6    QUOTExINDEX         CAC 40 Index, Equity Index, Euronext Paris   
7    QUOTExINDEX  CAC40 Eq Weight Index, Equity Index, Euronext ...   
8    QUOTExINDEX  CAC 40 Equal Weight NR Index, Equity Index, Eu...   
9    QUOTExINDEX   CAC 40 Short Index, Equity Index, Euronext Paris   

         PermID         PI       RIC  
0   71966969118    1033356     .FCHI  
1          <NA>   12308819   0#.FCHI  
2   69578169762   49801228    .PX1GR  
3   71037077632   49801208    .PX1NR  
4   72409723179  502602020    .CESGP  
5   55869215877   79709281    .CACEG  
6  130604903821     721346  .FCHISET  
7   55856795774   60996262    .CACEW  
8   55869215878   79708787    .CACEN  
9   55856795777   42171026    .CACSH

For indices, total return fields often do not work on the standard price-only RIC (like .FCHI for the CAC 40) because that RIC tracks price movements only and ignores dividends. The solution is to use the gross total return version of the index, which has a different RIC. For the CAC 40, the gross total return index is ".PX1GR".

From the index level we compute monthly returns using the .pct_change() method, which calculates the percentage change from one row to the next: return_t = (level_t / level_{t-1}) - 1.

In [26]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import pandas as pd  # pandas is the standard Python library for working with tabular data (DataFrames)
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
from datetime import datetime, date  # datetime provides date/time objects and parsing
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# Define the folder on your computer where the CSV file will be saved.
# IMPORTANT: change this path if you store your course files in a different location.
BASE_FOLDER = r"C:\Users\alexa\Documents\Research methods 2"  # variable assignment: store a value in 'BASE_FOLDER' so we can reuse it later

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
rd.open_session()

# Request the monthly level of the CAC 40 gross total return index.
# - universe=".PX1GR" tells Refinitiv which instrument we want (the CAC 40 gross total return index RIC).
# - fields=["TRDPRC_1"] asks for the last price (index level) at each date.
# - interval="monthly" asks for monthly observations instead of daily data.
# - start / end specify the date range of the history.
df = rd.get_history(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'df' so we can reuse it later
    universe=".PX1GR",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=["TRDPRC_1"],  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
    interval="monthly",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'interval' for the function call
    start="2005-01-01",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'start' for the function call
    end="2024-12-31",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'end' for the function call
) 

# Print a title so we can clearly see what is being displayed in the output.
print("=== CAC 40 gross total return index level (first rows) ===")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# df.head() shows the first 5 rows of the DataFrame 'df'.
# This is useful to quickly inspect that the data looks reasonable (dates, levels, column names, etc.).
print(df.head())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# Compute monthly returns as the percentage change between consecutive index levels.
# df.pct_change() computes (value_t / value_{t-1}) - 1 for each row.
# The first row has no previous observation, so pct_change() returns NaN there;
# .dropna() removes that first row with the missing value.
returns = df.pct_change().dropna()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'returns' so we can reuse it later

# Print a title for the second table so students can clearly distinguish levels from returns.
print("\n=== Monthly returns derived from the total return index (first rows) ===")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# returns.head() again shows the first 5 rows, now for the return series instead of the index level.
print(returns.head())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# Save the monthly returns to a CSV file on disk.
# returns.to_csv(...) writes the DataFrame 'returns' to a comma-separated text file.
# - The first argument is the full file path (folder + file name).
# - index=True keeps the date index as the first column in the CSV.
returns.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\cac40_gross_returns.csv", index=True)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk

# Close the Refinitiv session when we are done, to release resources and cleanly end the connection.
rd.close_session() 

=== CAC 40 gross total return index level (first rows) ===
.PX1GR      TRDPRC_1
Date                
2005-01-31   6318.85
2005-02-28   6502.05
2005-03-31   6567.64
2005-04-30   6329.85
2005-05-31    6774.6

=== Monthly returns derived from the total return index (first rows) ===
.PX1GR      TRDPRC_1
Date                
2005-02-28  0.028993
2005-03-31  0.010088
2005-04-30 -0.036206
2005-05-31  0.070262
2005-06-30  0.031456

Application. Now it is your turn to practice. Find the RIC for gross return of the EUROSTOXX 600 using the approach above and then retrieve yearly gross total returns over the last 20 years. A solution is provided below (click to unhide it).

In [25]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
rd.open_session()

# Search for "CAC40" in the INDEX_QUOTES view (indices only).
results = rd.discovery.search(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'results' so we can reuse it later
    query="STOXX 600",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'query' for the function call
    view=rd.discovery.Views.INDEX_QUOTES,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'view' for the function call
    top=10  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'top' for the function call
) 
print(results)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# Save search results to CSV.
results.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\search_600_index.csv", index=True)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk

rd.close_session()

In [24]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
rd.open_session()

# Download the monthly level of the CAC 40 gross total return index.
# TRDPRC_1 is the "last price" field commonly used for index levels.
df = rd.get_history(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'df' so we can reuse it later
    universe=".STOXX",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=["TRDPRC_1"],  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
    interval="1Y",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'interval' for the function call
    start="2005-01-01",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'start' for the function call
    end="2025-01-01"  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'end' for the function call
) 

# .pct_change() returns NaN for the first row (no previous level), so we drop it.
returns = df.pct_change().dropna()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'returns' so we can reuse it later

print("\n=== Yearly returns derived from the total return index (first rows) ===")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print(returns)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# Save search results to CSV.
returns.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\stox600_gross_returns.csv", index=True)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk

rd.close_session() 

=== Yearly returns derived from the total return index (first rows) ===
.STOXX      TRDPRC_1
Date

2007-12-31 -0.001697

2013-12-31  0.173699

2015-12-31  0.067934
2016-12-31 -0.012001
2017-12-31  0.076808
2018-12-31 -0.132407
2019-12-31  0.231571
2020-12-31 -0.040424
2021-12-31  0.222464
2022-12-31 -0.128967
2023-12-31  0.127327
2024-12-31  0.059772
2025-12-31  0.166601

2.6. Collecting accounting information for one stock¶

Using get_data(), we can collect accounting information for a given stock (over a given period).

In [20]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)

help(rd.get_data) 

Help on function get_data in module refinitiv.data._access_layer.get_data_func:

get_data(universe: Union[str, Iterable[str]], fields: Union[str, Iterable[str], NoneType] = None, parameters: Union[str, dict, NoneType] = None, use_field_names_in_headers: bool = None) -> pandas.core.frame.DataFrame
    Retrieves pricing snapshots, as well as Fundamental and Reference data.

    Parameters
    ----------
    universe: str | list
        Instruments to request
    fields: str | list, optional
        Fields to request
    parameters: str | dict, optional
        Single key=value global parameter or dictionary of global parameters to request
    use_field_names_in_headers: bool, default False
        If True - returns field name as column headers for data instead of title

    Returns
    -------
    pandas.DataFrame

    Examples
    --------
    >>> get_data(universe=['IBM.N', 'VOD.L'], fields=['BID', 'ASK'])
    >>> get_data(
    ...     universe=['GOOG.O', 'AAPL.O'],
    ...     fields=['TR.EV','TR.EVToSales'],
    ...     parameters = {'SDate': '0CY', 'Curn': 'CAD'}
    ...)

As before, we look up for the right variable name in Refinitiv using either:

  • Data Item Browser (DIB) — type DIB in the Workspace search bar.
  • CodeCreator (CODECR) — type CODECR in the Workspace search bar.

Doing so we find that for a stock such as TESLA, the right variable name for net income is TR.NetIncome. We retrieve it over the last ten years.

In [31]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
rd.open_session()

# Define identifier
ric = "TSLA.O"  # variable assignment: store a value in 'ric' so we can reuse it later

# Define the sample period and frequency.
# SDate / EDate = start / end; Frq = "FY" means one observation per fiscal year.
params = {  # variable assignment: store a value in 'params' so we can reuse it later
    "SDate": "2014-01-01", 
    "EDate": "2024-12-31", 
    "Frq":   "FY" 
} 

# Define the raw accounting fields to download.
# Field mnemonics must always be verified in the Data Item Browser (DIB).
fields = ["TR.NetIncome", "TR.NetIncome.Date"]  # variable assignment: store a value in 'fields' so we can reuse it later

# Download the panel using rd.get_data().
tesla_data = rd.get_data(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'tesla_data' so we can reuse it later
    universe=ric,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=fields,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
    parameters=params,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'parameters' for the function call
    use_field_names_in_headers=True  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'use_field_names_in_headers' for the function call
) 

# set data as in dex
tesla_data.index = tesla_data["TR.NETINCOME.DATE"] 
tesla_data = tesla_data[["TR.NETINCOME"]]  # variable assignment: store a value in 'tesla_data' so we can reuse it later

# show data
print(tesla_data)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# save data
tesla_data.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER+"\\tesla_net_income.csv")  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk

                   TR.NETINCOME
TR.NETINCOME.DATE              
2013-12-31            -74014000
2014-12-31           -294040000
2015-12-31           -888663000

2017-12-31          -1961400000
2018-12-31           -976091000
2019-12-31           -870000000
2020-12-31            690000000
2021-12-31           5524000000
2022-12-31          12583000000
2023-12-31          14999000000

Application. Now it is your turn to practice. Collect the capital expenditures of the company Dassault over the last five years. First, find the RIC for Dassault, then the variable name for the capital expenditures, finally collect. A solution is provided below (click to unhide it).

In [32]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
rd.open_session()

# Define identifier
ric = "DAST.PA"  # variable assignment: store a value in 'ric' so we can reuse it later

# Define the sample period and frequency.
# SDate / EDate = start / end; Frq = "FY" means one observation per fiscal year.
params = {  # variable assignment: store a value in 'params' so we can reuse it later
    "SDate": "-5Y", 
    "EDate": "0", 
    "Frq":   "FY" 
} 

# Define the raw accounting fields to download.
# Field mnemonics must always be verified in the Data Item Browser (DIB).
fields = ["TR.CapitalExpenditures", "TR.CapitalExpenditures.Date"]  # variable assignment: store a value in 'fields' so we can reuse it later

# Download the panel using rd.get_data().
dassault_data = rd.get_data(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'dassault_data' so we can reuse it later
    universe=ric,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=fields,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
    parameters=params,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'parameters' for the function call
    use_field_names_in_headers=True  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'use_field_names_in_headers' for the function call
) 

# set data as in dex
dassault_data.index = dassault_data["TR.CAPITALEXPENDITURES.DATE"] 
dassault_data = dassault_data[["TR.CAPITALEXPENDITURES"]]  # variable assignment: store a value in 'dassault_data' so we can reuse it later

# show data
print(dassault_data)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# save data
dassault_data.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER+"\\dassault_capex.csv")  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk

                             TR.CAPITALEXPENDITURES
TR.CAPITALEXPENDITURES.DATE                        
2021-12-31                               -103700000
2022-12-31                               -132300000
2023-12-31                               -145300000
2024-12-31                               -193400000
2025-12-31                               -160500000

2.7. Collecting accounting information for all CAC 40 constituents¶

We now build a panel of accounting variables for all the constituents of the CAC 40 over the last 20 years. A panel (also called longitudinal data) is a dataset where we observe the same set of companies across multiple time periods — here one row per company per fiscal year.

The steps are:

  1. Identify the CAC 40 constituents. We use the index chain RIC 0#.FCHI. We already know that the RIC of the index CAC40 is .FCHI, the 0# prefix is Refinitiv's Chain RIC notation. It tells the system to expand the index into its individual constituents rather than returning the index itself.
  2. Set the time span and frequency. We request 20 years of annual (fiscal-year) data using the parameters argument: SDate (start date), EDate (end date), and Frq (frequency, "FY" for fiscal year).
  3. Request fundamental fields. We use rd.get_data() — designed for fundamental / reference data — rather than rd.get_history() (which is for time-series / market data). The fields requested are the raw accounting items we need to construct standard financial ratios.
  4. Construct clean variables in Python. We rename columns to readable names and compute standard ratios: ROA (Return on Assets), leverage (Debt / Assets), market-to-book, capex intensity, R&D intensity, and log of total assets (a proxy for firm size).
  5. Save the panel as a CSV file so that later sections can reload it without re-downloading.

The cell below first retrieves the current CAC 40 constituent list.

In [33]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
rd.open_session()

# Retrieve the current CAC 40 constituents from the index chain RIC.
# '0#.FCHI' is the chain RIC that lists every member of the CAC 40.
cac_chain = "0#.FCHI"
constituents = rd.get_data(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'constituents' so we can reuse it later
    universe=cac_chain,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=["TR.RIC", "TR.CommonName"]  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
) 

print(constituents)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print(f"Number of constituents in {cac_chain}: {len(constituents)}")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# Save the constituent list to CSV.
constituents.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\cac40_constituents.csv", index=True)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk

rd.close_session()

Number of constituents in 0#.FCHI: 40

The next cell downloads the 20-year accounting panel and constructs the derived variables. This cell depends on the constituents DataFrame created just above — they must be run in order.

Fields requested (raw accounting items):

Field mnemonic Description Variable name after rename
TR.NetIncome Net income net_income
TR.CompanyMarketCap Market capitalisation market_cap
TR.F.ComEqTot Common equity (book value) equity_value
TR.CapitalExpenditures Capital expenditures capex
TR.TotalAssets Total assets total_assets
TR.RnDExp Research & development expense rd_exp
TR.TotalDebt Total debt total_debt
TR.TotalAssets.date Fiscal period end date period_end

Derived ratios:

Variable Formula Interpretation
ROA Net income / Total assets How efficiently a company uses its assets to generate profit
Leverage Total debt / Total assets How much of the company's assets are financed by debt
Market-to-book Market cap / Equity book value How much investors value the company relative to its accounting value
Capex / Assets Capital expenditures / Total assets Investment intensity
R&D / Assets R&D expense / Total assets Innovation intensity
ln(Total assets) Natural logarithm of total assets Firm size (log-transformed to reduce skewness)
In [ ]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import pandas as pd  # pandas is the standard Python library for working with tabular data (DataFrames)
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
from datetime import datetime, date  # datetime provides date/time objects and parsing

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
rd.open_session()

# Extract the list of RICs from the constituents table.
rics = constituents["RIC"].tolist()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'rics' so we can reuse it later
print(f"Downloading fundamentals for {len(rics)} CAC40 constituents…")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# Define the sample period and frequency.
# SDate / EDate = start / end; Frq = "FY" means one observation per fiscal year.
params = {  # variable assignment: store a value in 'params' so we can reuse it later
    "SDate": "2005-01-01", 
    "EDate": "2024-12-31", 
    "Frq":   "FY" 
} 

# Define the raw accounting fields to download.
# Field mnemonics must always be verified in the Data Item Browser (DIB).
fields = [  # variable assignment: store a value in 'fields' so we can reuse it later
    "TR.NetIncome", 
    "TR.CompanyMarketCap", 
    "TR.F.ComEqTot", 
    "TR.CapitalExpenditures", 
    "TR.TotalAssets", 
    "TR.RnDExp", 
    "TR.TotalDebt", 
    "TR.TotalAssets.date" 
] 

# Download the panel using rd.get_data().
raw_panel = rd.get_data(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'raw_panel' so we can reuse it later
    universe=rics,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=fields,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
    parameters=params,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'parameters' for the function call
    use_field_names_in_headers=True  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'use_field_names_in_headers' for the function call
) 

rd.close_session() 
print(raw_panel.head())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

In [35]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import numpy as np  # NumPy provides fast numerical arrays and math functions
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder
# ── Rename columns to readable names ──────────────────────────────────────────
rename_candidates = {  # variable assignment: store a value in 'rename_candidates' so we can reuse it later
    "TR.NETINCOME":            "net_income", 
    "TR.COMPANYMARKETCAP":     "market_cap", 
    "TR.F.COMEQTOT":           "equity_value", 
    "TR.CAPITALEXPENDITURES":  "capex", 
    "TR.TOTALASSETS":          "total_assets", 
    "TR.RNDEXP":               "rd_exp", 
    "TR.TOTALDEBT":            "total_debt", 
    "TR.TOTALASSETS.DATE":     "period_end" 
} 

panel = raw_panel.rename(columns=rename_candidates)  # variable assignment: store a value in 'panel' so we can reuse it later

# ── Compute derived financial ratios ──────────────────────────────────────────
panel["ROA"]            = panel["net_income"]  / panel["total_assets"] 
panel["capex_at"]       = panel["capex"]       / panel["total_assets"] 
panel["ln_at"]          = np.log(panel["total_assets"]) 
panel["leverage"]       = panel["total_debt"]  / panel["total_assets"] 
panel["Market_to_book"] = panel["market_cap"]  / panel["equity_value"] 
panel["rnd_at"]         = panel["rd_exp"]      / panel["total_assets"] 

# Quick inspection
print(panel.head())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print("\nPanel shape (rows, columns):", panel.shape)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# Save the panel as a CSV for reuse in later sections.
panel.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\cac40_fundamentals_2005_2024.csv", index=True)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk

  Instrument  net_income          market_cap   equity_value        capex  \

       ln_at  leverage  Market_to_book  rnd_at

Application. Now it is your turn to practice. For all the constituent of the EURO STOXX 600, retrieve the sales as of 2024. A solution is provided below (click to unhide it).

In [ ]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
rd.open_session()

# Retrieve constituents of STOXX 
constituents = rd.get_data(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'constituents' so we can reuse it later
    universe=["0#.STOXX"],
    fields=["TR.RIC", "TR.CommonName"]  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
) 
print(constituents)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# Save the constituent list to CSV.
constituents.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\stoxx_constituents.csv", index=True)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk

# Extract the list of RICs from the constituents table.
rics = constituents["RIC"].tolist()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'rics' so we can reuse it later
#keep non void rics
rics = list(filter(None, rics))
print(rics)

# Define the sample period and frequency.
# SDate / EDate = start / end; Frq = "FY" means one observation per fiscal year.
params = {  # variable assignment: store a value in 'params' so we can reuse it later
    "EDate": "2024-12-31", 
} 

# Define the raw accounting fields to download.
# Field mnemonics must always be verified in the Data Item Browser (DIB).
fields = ["TR.Revenue", "TR.Revenue.Date"]  # variable assignment: store a value in 'fields' so we can reuse it later

# Download the panel using rd.get_data().
raw_panel = rd.get_data(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'raw_panel' so we can reuse it later
    universe=rics,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=fields,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
    parameters=params,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'parameters' for the function call
    use_field_names_in_headers=True  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'use_field_names_in_headers' for the function call
) 

print(raw_panel.head())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
raw_panel.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\stoxx_sales_2024.csv", index=True)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk

rd.close_session()

2.8. Downloading monthly total returns for all CAC 40 constituents¶

In Section 2.7 we built a panel of annual accounting variables. For many empirical applications we also need stock returns. In this subsection we download monthly total returns (including dividends) for every CAC 40 stock over the same 2005–2024 window.

We use rd.get_history() (for time-series data) instead of rd.get_data() (which is for snapshot / fundamental data). The field TR.TotalReturn1Mo gives the one-month total return — it includes both price changes and reinvested dividends, which is what we typically want when studying portfolio performance or testing asset-pricing models.

This cell depends on the constituents DataFrame created in Section 2.6. We load the csv file that we have previously created cac40_constituent.csv.

In [45]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import pandas as pd  # pandas is the standard Python library for working with tabular data (DataFrames)
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
rd.open_session()

# Reload the CAC 40 constituent list from the CSV saved in Section 2.6.
constituents = pd.read_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\cac40_constituents.csv")  # variable assignment: store a value in 'constituents' so we can reuse it later
rics = constituents["RIC"].tolist()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'rics' so we can reuse it later

# Download monthly total returns (including dividends).
returns_cac40 = rd.get_history(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'returns_cac40' so we can reuse it later
    universe=rics,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=["TR.TotalReturn1Mo"],  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
    interval="monthly",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'interval' for the function call
    start="2005-01-01",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'start' for the function call
    end="2024-12-31"  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'end' for the function call
) 

print("=== Monthly total returns (first rows) ===")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print(returns_cac40.head())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print("\nShape of returns panel (rows, columns):", returns_cac40.shape)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# Save to CSV.
returns_cac40.to_csv(  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk
    BASE_FOLDER + "\\cac40_monthly_total_returns_2005_2024.csv", index=True 
) 

rd.close_session()

Application. Now it is your turn to practice. For all the constituent of the S&P 500, collect the time series of monthly returns over the year 2024. A solution is provided below (click to unhide it).

In [46]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
rd.open_session()

constituents = rd.get_data(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'constituents' so we can reuse it later
    universe="0#.SPX",
    fields=["TR.RIC", "TR.CommonName"]  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
) 

print(constituents)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# Save the constituent list to CSV.
constituents.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\sp500_constituents.csv", index=True)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk

rd.close_session()

4          A.N       A.N              Agilent Technologies Inc
..         ...       ...                                   ...

499   GOOGL.OQ  GOOGL.OQ                          Alphabet Inc

In [47]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import pandas as pd  # pandas is the standard Python library for working with tabular data (DataFrames)
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
rd.open_session()

# Get constituent
constituents = pd.read_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\sp500_constituents.csv")  # variable assignment: store a value in 'constituents' so we can reuse it later
rics = constituents["RIC"].tolist()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'rics' so we can reuse it later

# Download monthly total returns (including dividends).
returns_sp500 = rd.get_history(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'returns_sp500' so we can reuse it later
    universe=rics,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=["TR.TotalReturn1Mo"],  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
    interval="monthly",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'interval' for the function call
    start="2024-01-01",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'start' for the function call
    end="2024-12-31"  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'end' for the function call
) 

print(returns_sp500.head())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# Save to CSV.
returns_sp500.to_csv(  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk
    BASE_FOLDER + "\\sp500_monthly_total_returns_2024.csv", index=True 
) 

rd.close_session()
