2.9. Extracting earnings forecasts (EPS) for a single stock¶

Earnings Per Share (EPS) is a company's net profit divided by the number of shares outstanding. Analysts regularly publish forecasts of future EPS — these "consensus" forecasts are important because they reflect the market's expectations about a company's profitability.

Refinitiv provides consensus (summary) EPS forecasts through dedicated fields. Key concepts:

  • TR.EPSMean — The average (consensus) EPS forecast across all analysts covering the stock.
  • TR.EPSActValue — The actual (realised) EPS once the company reports earnings.
  • Period parameter — Controls the forecast horizon: "FY1" = next fiscal year, "FY2" = the year after.
  • Frq parameter — Controls how often the forecast is sampled: "M" = monthly.

The cell below downloads monthly EPS consensus forecasts and actuals for Microsoft (MSFT.O) at two horizons.

In this code, we use a function. A function is a reusable block of code that you define once and can then call as many times as you want with different inputs, without copy-pasting the same lines over and over. Think of it like a recipe: you write the instructions once, and then every time you want to cook the dish, you just say "make the recipe" and provide the specific ingredients.

You create a function with the def keyword, give it a name, and list its parameters (inputs) in parentheses — for example "def get_eps_forecasts(ric, period):". Everything indented below def is the function's body: the instructions it executes each time you call it. The return statement at the end sends the result back to you. Once defined, you call the function by writing its name and passing actual values, for instance "eps_fy1 = get_eps_forecasts("MSFT.O", "FY1")". Python then runs the entire body with "ric = 'MSFT.O'" and " period = 'FY1' ", and stores the returned DataFrame in "*eps_fy1". This avoids copy-pasting the same code every time you want to repeat an operation with different inputs.

In [48]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
from datetime import datetime, date  # datetime provides date/time objects and parsing
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
rd.open_session()

# define ric
ric = "MSFT.O"  # Microsoft RIC

# Common date range and monthly frequency for the EPS forecast history.
def get_eps_forecasts(ric, period): 
    """
    Download EPS consensus forecast and actual for a given RIC and horizon.
    'period' should be 'FY1' (next fiscal year) or 'FY2' (year after next).
    """ 
    params = {  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'params' for the function call
    "SDate": "2005-01-01", 
    "EDate": "2024-12-31", 
    "Frq":   "M"             # monthly frequency
    } 
    params["Period"] = period 

    fields = [  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
        "TR.EPSMean",          # consensus EPS forecast (mean across analysts)
        "TR.EPSMean.Date",     # date associated with the forecast
        "TR.EPSActValue"       # actual (realised) EPS
    ] 

    df = rd.get_data(  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'df' for the function call
        universe=[ric],  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
        fields=fields,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
        parameters=params,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'parameters' for the function call
        use_field_names_in_headers=True  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'use_field_names_in_headers' for the function call
    ) 
    return df 

# Retrieve EPS forecasts for two horizons.
eps_fy1 = get_eps_forecasts(ric, "FY1")  # variable assignment: store a value in 'eps_fy1' so we can reuse it later
eps_fy2 = get_eps_forecasts(ric, "FY2")  # variable assignment: store a value in 'eps_fy2' so we can reuse it later

print(f"=== EPS consensus and actuals for {ric} (FY1) ===")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print(eps_fy1.head())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print(f"\n=== EPS consensus and actuals for {ric} (FY2) ===")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print(eps_fy2.head())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# Save both tables to CSV.
eps_fy1.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\msft_eps_fy1_monthly.csv", index=True)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk
eps_fy2.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\msft_eps_fy2_monthly.csv", index=True)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk

rd.close_session() 

=== EPS consensus and actuals for MSFT.O (FY1) ===
  Instrument  TR.EPSMEAN TR.EPSMEAN.DATE  TR.EPSACTVALUE
0     MSFT.O     1.28345      2005-01-31             1.3
1     MSFT.O     1.28357      2005-02-14             1.3
2     MSFT.O     1.28536      2005-03-31             1.3
3     MSFT.O     1.29321      2005-04-29             1.3
4     MSFT.O     1.29419      2005-05-09             1.3

=== EPS consensus and actuals for MSFT.O (FY2) ===
  Instrument  TR.EPSMEAN TR.EPSMEAN.DATE  TR.EPSACTVALUE
0     MSFT.O     1.40278      2005-01-31            1.27
1     MSFT.O     1.40412      2005-02-14            1.27
2     MSFT.O     1.40059      2005-03-31            1.27
3     MSFT.O     1.36571      2005-04-29            1.27
4     MSFT.O     1.34833      2005-05-20            1.27

2.10. Extracting FX and crypto time series¶

In this subsection we show how to extract foreign exchange (FX) and cryptocurrency time series. The pattern is the same as before: we use rd.get_history(), specify the instrument RIC, choose the frequency, and select the fields.

For FX, we use standard currency RICs (for example "EUR=" for the EUR/USD exchange rate). For cryptocurrencies, you must first look up the correct RIC in Refinitiv Workspace or DIB (for example by searching for "Bitcoin").

We request only the BID (price a buyer offers) and ASK (price a seller accepts) fields and then compute the bid-ask spread (= ASK − BID) in Python. The spread measures the transaction cost of trading: a smaller spread means a more liquid instrument.

The first cell retrieves daily and intraday (1-minute) FX data for EUR/USD. For intraday data, rd.get_history() accepts relative time ranges via Python's timedelta object (for example, timedelta(days=-5) means "5 days ago").

In [50]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
from datetime import datetime, date, timedelta # datetime provides date/time objects and parsing
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
rd.open_session()

# ric 
fx_ric = "EUR="  # EUR/USD exchange rate RIC

# ── Daily BID and ASK for a full calendar year ────────────────────────────────
fx_daily = rd.get_history(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'fx_daily' so we can reuse it later
    universe=fx_ric,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=["BID", "ASK"],  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
    interval="daily",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'interval' for the function call
    start="2024-01-01",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'start' for the function call
    end="2024-12-31"  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'end' for the function call
) 

# ── Intraday 1-minute data for the last 5 days ───────────────────────────────
# timedelta(days=-5) means "5 calendar days before now".
fx_1min = rd.get_history(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'fx_1min' so we can reuse it later
    universe=fx_ric,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=["BID", "ASK"],  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
    interval="1min",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'interval' for the function call
    start=timedelta(days=-5),  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'start' for the function call
    end=timedelta(days=0)  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'end' for the function call
) 

# Compute the bid-ask spread = ASK − BID.
if {"ASK", "BID"}.issubset(fx_daily.columns):  # if-statement: run the indented block only if the condition is True
    fx_daily["SPREAD"] = fx_daily["ASK"] - fx_daily["BID"] 

if not fx_1min.empty and {"ASK", "BID"}.issubset(fx_1min.columns):  # if-statement: run the indented block only if the condition is True
    fx_1min["SPREAD"] = fx_1min["ASK"] - fx_1min["BID"] 

print("=== EUR= daily FX data (BID, ASK, SPREAD) ===")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print(fx_daily.head())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print("\n=== EUR= 1-minute FX data (BID, ASK, SPREAD) ===")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print(fx_1min.head())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# Save to CSV.
fx_daily.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\eur_fx_daily_2024.csv", index=True)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk
fx_1min.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\eur_fx_1min_last5d.csv", index=True)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk

rd.close_session() 

=== EUR= daily FX data (BID, ASK, SPREAD) ===
EUR=           BID     ASK  SPREAD
Date                              
2024-01-02  1.0947  1.0951  0.0004
2024-01-03  1.0921  1.0922  0.0001
2024-01-04  1.0943  1.0947  0.0004
2024-01-05  1.0941  1.0943  0.0002
2024-01-08  1.0949  1.0953  0.0004

=== EUR= 1-minute FX data (BID, ASK, SPREAD) ===
EUR=                    BID     ASK  SPREAD
Timestamp                                  
2026-02-23 12:49:00  1.1778   1.178  0.0002
2026-02-23 12:50:00  1.1779   1.178  0.0001
2026-02-23 12:51:00   1.178  1.1782  0.0002
2026-02-23 12:52:00  1.1781  1.1783  0.0002
2026-02-23 12:53:00  1.1782  1.1783  0.0001

The next cell repeats the same pattern for a cryptocurrency (Bitcoin, "BTC="). Replace the RIC with the correct one from Refinitiv Workspace if needed.

In [51]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
rd.open_session()

# Replace this with the actual crypto RIC from Refinitiv Workspace / DIB.
crypto_ric = "BTC="  # variable assignment: store a value in 'crypto_ric' so we can reuse it later

# Download daily BID and ASK for Bitcoin over 2024.
crypto_daily = rd.get_history(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'crypto_daily' so we can reuse it later
    universe=crypto_ric,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=["BID", "ASK"],  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
    interval="daily",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'interval' for the function call
    start="2024-01-01",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'start' for the function call
    end="2024-12-31"  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'end' for the function call
) 

# Compute the bid-ask spread.
crypto_daily["SPREAD"] = crypto_daily["ASK"] - crypto_daily["BID"] 

print(f"=== Crypto daily data for {crypto_ric} (BID, ASK, SPREAD) ===")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print(crypto_daily.head())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# Save to CSV.
crypto_daily.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\crypto_daily_2024.csv", index=True)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk

rd.close_session() 

=== Crypto daily data for BTC= (BID, ASK, SPREAD) ===
BTC=             BID       ASK  SPREAD
Date                                  
2024-01-02   45124.0   45125.0     1.0
2024-01-03   42939.0   42949.0    10.0
2024-01-04   44488.0   44490.0     2.0
2024-01-05  43960.11  43961.74    1.63
2024-01-06   43919.0   43920.0     1.0

2.11. Extracting ESG information for companies¶

ESG stands for Environmental, Social, and Governance — three broad categories used to evaluate a company's behaviour beyond pure financial performance. Refinitiv provides ESG scores that summarise how well a company performs on each dimension.

Key fields:

Field Description
TR.TRESGScore Overall ESG score (0–100) based on reported data
TR.TRESGCScore Combined ESG score, discounted for significant controversies
TR.EnvironmentPillarScore Environmental pillar score
TR.SocialPillarScore Social pillar score
TR.GovernancePillarScore Governance pillar score
TR.ControvPrivacy Example controversy metric (privacy-related)

The cell below retrieves a 10-year ESG history and a controversy metric for Microsoft (MSFT.O). The same pattern works for any RIC or list of RICs.

In [52]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
from datetime import datetime, date  # datetime provides date/time objects and parsing
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
rd.open_session()

ric_esg = "MSFT.O"  # variable assignment: store a value in 'ric_esg' so we can reuse it later

# ── (a) 10-year ESG and pillar score history (fiscal-year frequency) ──────────
esg_history = rd.get_data(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'esg_history' so we can reuse it later
    universe=[ric_esg],  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=[  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
        "TR.TRESGScore",              # overall ESG score
        "TR.TRESGScore.date",         # associated date
        "TR.TRESGCScore",             # combined (controversy-adjusted) score
        "TR.EnvironmentPillarScore", 
        "TR.SocialPillarScore", 
        "TR.GovernancePillarScore", 
    ], 
    parameters={"SDate": "2015-01-01", "EDate": "2024-12-31", "Frq": "FY"},  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'parameters' for the function call
    use_field_names_in_headers=True,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'use_field_names_in_headers' for the function call
) 

print(f"=== 10-year ESG and pillar score history for {ric_esg} ===")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print(esg_history)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# ── (b) Controversy metrics ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
controversy_history = rd.get_data(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'controversy_history' so we can reuse it later
    universe=[ric_esg],  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=["TR.ControvPrivacy", "TR.ControvPrivacy.Date"],  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
    parameters={"SDate": "2015-01-01", "EDate": "2024-12-31", "Frq": "FY"},  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'parameters' for the function call
    use_field_names_in_headers=True,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'use_field_names_in_headers' for the function call
) 

print(f"\n=== Privacy controversy metric for {ric_esg} ===")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print(controversy_history)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# Save to CSV.
esg_history.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\msft_esg_history_2015_2024.csv", index=True)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk
controversy_history.to_csv(  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk
    BASE_FOLDER + "\\msft_esg_controversies_2015_2024.csv", index=True 
) 

rd.close_session()

Application. Now it is your turn to practice. For all the constituent of the CAC40, collect the time series of yearly total carbon emission (scope 1, 2, 3) over the last five years. First, search for the right variable name in Refinitiv. A solution is provided below (click to unhide it).

In [53]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import pandas as pd  # pandas is the standard Python library for working with tabular data (DataFrames)
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
rd.open_session()

# Get constituent
constituents = pd.read_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\cac40_constituents.csv")  # variable assignment: store a value in 'constituents' so we can reuse it later
rics = constituents["RIC"].tolist()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'rics' so we can reuse it later

# ── (a) 10-year ESG and pillar score history (fiscal-year frequency) ──────────
co2_history = rd.get_data(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'co2_history' so we can reuse it later
    universe=rics,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=["TR.AnalyticEstimatedCO2Total", "TR.AnalyticEstimatedCO2Total.Date"],  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
    parameters={"SDate": "2020-01-01", "EDate": "2024-12-31", "Frq": "FY"},  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'parameters' for the function call
    use_field_names_in_headers=True,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'use_field_names_in_headers' for the function call
) 

print(co2_history)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# Save to CSV.
co2_history.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\cac40_co2.csv", index=True)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk

rd.close_session() 

    Instrument  TR.ANALYTICESTIMATEDCO2TOTAL TR.ANALYTICESTIMATEDCO2TOTAL.DATE

1      SOGN.PA                        121935                        2021-12-31
2      SOGN.PA                        110270                        2022-12-31
3      SOGN.PA                        100452                        2023-12-31
4      SOGN.PA                        153745                        2024-12-31
..         ...                           ...                               ...
192    MICP.PA                       2793530                        2020-12-31
193    MICP.PA                       3226690                        2021-12-31
194    MICP.PA                       3002140                        2022-12-31
195    MICP.PA                       3085010                        2023-12-31
196    MICP.PA                       2929860                        2024-12-31

[197 rows x 3 columns]

2.12. Extracting fund information¶

Refinitiv provides detailed data on mutual funds and ETFs (Exchange-Traded Funds), including fund identification, investment objectives, performance over various horizons, risk metrics, and portfolio holdings. The exact fields available depend on your institution's subscription.

We search for funds using the fund screener or type the name of find in the Refinitiv search bar.

The cell below retrieves identification, performance, risk, and holdings data for an example fund (Mirova Global Sustainability Fund, Lipper ID LP68603610). We also request a 10-year rolling monthly performance series.

For full documentation on rd.get_data() parameters, see the official LSEG reference or call help(rd.get_data).

In [54]:
import pandas as pd  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell
from refinitiv.data.content import search  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell
import refinitiv.data as rd  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell


rd.open_session()  # rd.open_session() starts an authenticated connection to Refinitiv/LSEG (needed before queries)

# Retrieve metadata for the fund view.
meta = search.metadata.Definition(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'meta' so we can reuse it later
    view=search.Views.FUND_QUOTES  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'view' for the function call
).get_data() 

# Save full metadata and show searchable / exact columns.
meta.data.df.to_csv("fund_view_metadata.csv", index=True)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk
print(meta.data.df[["Type", "Searchable", "Exact"]].to_string())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

rd.close_session()

How do I know possible values for one variable (or more) from one view to then put in place a screen/filter? I can use a navigator. Navigators provide the ability to summarize the distribution of your results. They are particularly useful when you are interested in gathering the domain of values for a specific property. or simply to understand how to build your filters based on specific values contained within Properties. Navigators can be used against a specific View, used in conjunction with either a query, a filter, or both. Navigators can be simple or very powerful, but provide a very useful way to capture results in logical buckets.

In [55]:
import pandas as pd  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell
from refinitiv.data.content import search  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell
import refinitiv.data as rd  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell

rd.open_session()  # rd.open_session() starts an authenticated connection to Refinitiv/LSEG (needed before queries)

response = search.Definition(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'response' so we can reuse it later
    top=0,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'top' for the function call
    navigators="AssetCategoryName"  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'navigators' for the function call
).get_data() 

rd.close_session() 

response.data.raw['Navigators']['AssetCategoryName']

In [56]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
rd.open_session()  # rd.open_session() starts an authenticated connection to Refinitiv/LSEG (needed before queries)

# Search for fund called mirova something
fund_list = rd.discovery.search(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'fund_list' so we can reuse it later
    view=rd.discovery.Views.FUND_QUOTES,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'view' for the function call
    query="Mirova",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'query' for the function call
    top=100  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'top' for the function call
) 

# Save to CSV and display the first rows.
fund_list.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\fund_list_FR.csv", index=True)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk
fund_list.head(10)

7 QUOTExFUND Mirova Emploi France D, Open-End Fund, EUR, Li... 55867804404 53725501 LP65119990

In [57]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
from datetime import datetime, date  # datetime provides date/time objects and parsing
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

rd.open_session()  # rd.open_session() starts an authenticated connection to Refinitiv/LSEG (needed before queries)

# Example fund: Mirova Global Green Bond Fund 
fund_universe = ["LP68234916"]  # variable assignment: store a value in 'fund_universe' so we can reuse it later

# ── (a) Fund identification and performance fields ────────────────────────────
fund_fields = [  # variable assignment: store a value in 'fund_fields' so we can reuse it later
    "TR.FundName",              # fund name
    "TR.FundObjective",         # stated investment objective
    "TR.FundType",              # fund type (equity, bond, mixed, etc.)
    "TR.FundGeographicFocus",   # geographic focus
    "TR.FundLaunchDate",        # date the fund was launched
    "TR.FundTER",               # Total Expense Ratio
    "TR.FundAlpha1Year",        # Jensen's alpha over 1 year
    "TR.FundAlpha3Year", 
    "TR.FundAlpha5Year", 
    "TR.FundRelPerf6Mth",       # relative performance vs benchmark
    "TR.FundRelPerf1Year", 
    "TR.FundRelPerf3Year", 
    "TR.FundRelPerf5Year", 
    "TR.FundRelPerf10Year", 
    "TR.FundStdDev1Year",       # standard deviation (volatility) over 1 year
    "TR.FundStdDev3Year", 
    "TR.FundStdDev5Year", 
    "TR.FundStdDev10Year", 
] 

fund_info = rd.get_data(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'fund_info' so we can reuse it later
    universe=fund_universe,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=fund_fields,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
    use_field_names_in_headers=True  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'use_field_names_in_headers' for the function call
) 

print("=== Fund information ===")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print(fund_info.T)   # transpose so each field appears as a row
fund_info.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\fund_information_example.csv", index=True)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk

rd.close_session()

TR.FUNDOBJECTIVE        The Fund will invest principally in bonds, amo...
TR.FUNDTYPE                                                          Bond
TR.FUNDGEOGRAPHICFOCUS                                             Europe
TR.FUNDLAUNCHDATE                                     2013-10-15 00:00:00
TR.FUNDTER                                                         0.6045
TR.FUNDALPHA1YEAR                                                0.040972
TR.FUNDALPHA3YEAR                                                0.044346
TR.FUNDALPHA5YEAR                                                0.005621
TR.FUNDRELPERF1YEAR                                              0.494998
TR.FUNDRELPERF3YEAR                                              1.783598
TR.FUNDRELPERF5YEAR                                             -0.930283
TR.FUNDRELPERF10YEAR                                            -0.054308
TR.FUNDSTDDEV1YEAR                                               2.951064

TR.FUNDSTDDEV5YEAR                                               6.527467
TR.FUNDSTDDEV10YEAR                                               5.70924
In [ ]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
from datetime import datetime, date  # datetime provides date/time objects and parsing
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
rd.open_session()  # rd.open_session() starts an authenticated connection to Refinitiv/LSEG (needed before queries)

# Example fund: Mirova Global Sustainability Fund (Lipper ID).
fund_universe = ["LP68603610"]  # variable assignment: store a value in 'fund_universe' so we can reuse it later

# ── (b) Fund holdings ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
fund_holdings = rd.get_data(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'fund_holdings' so we can reuse it later
    universe=fund_universe,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=[  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
        "TR.FundHoldingRIC", 
        "TR.FundHoldingName", 
        "TR.FundNumberOfShares", 
        "TR.FundPercentageOfFundAssets" 
    ], 
    use_field_names_in_headers=True,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'use_field_names_in_headers' for the function call
) 

print(f"\n=== Fund holdings as of {date.today().isoformat()} ===")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print(fund_holdings.head(10))  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
fund_holdings.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\fund_holdings.csv", index=True)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk

rd.close_session()

In [58]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
from datetime import datetime, date  # datetime provides date/time objects and parsing
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
rd.open_session()  # rd.open_session() starts an authenticated connection to Refinitiv/LSEG (needed before queries)

# Example fund: Mirova Global Sustainability Fund (Lipper ID).
fund_universe = ["LP68603610"]  # variable assignment: store a value in 'fund_universe' so we can reuse it later

# ── (c) 10-year rolling monthly performance ──────────────────────────────────
fund_perf = rd.get_history(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'fund_perf' so we can reuse it later
    universe=fund_universe[0],  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=["TR.FundRollingPerformance(RollTimeFrame=10Y,Interval=M)"]  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
) 

print("\n=== Fund rolling monthly performance (first rows) ===")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print(fund_perf.head(10))  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
fund_perf.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\fund_monthly_performance.csv", index=True)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk

rd.close_session()

2020-12-31             4.478861

2.13. Extracting M&A deal information¶

Refinitiv maintains a comprehensive Mergers & Acquisitions (M&A) deals database. We can search and filter deals using the DEALS_MERGERS_AND_ACQUISITIONS Search view.

Before building a query, it is useful to inspect the metadata of the view to see all available properties, their types, and whether they can be used as filters. The first cell below does this. The second cell then runs an actual deal search.

For detailed Python examples, see the LSEG search tutorials: Search with the Data Library.

In [59]:
import pandas as pd  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell
from refinitiv.data.content import search  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell
import refinitiv.data as rd  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell

rd.open_session()  # rd.open_session() starts an authenticated connection to Refinitiv/LSEG (needed before queries)

# Retrieve metadata for the Deals M&A view.
meta = search.metadata.Definition(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'meta' so we can reuse it later
    view=search.Views.DEALS_MERGERS_AND_ACQUISITIONS  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'view' for the function call
).get_data() 

# Save full metadata and show searchable / exact columns.
meta.data.df.to_csv("deals_view_metadata.csv", index=True)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk
print(meta.data.df[["Type", "Searchable", "Exact"]].to_string())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

rd.close_session()

How do I know possible values for one variable (or more) from one view to then put in place a screen/filter? I can use a navigator. Navigators provide the ability to summarize the distribution of your results. They are particularly useful when you are interested in gathering the domain of values for a specific property. or simply to understand how to build your filters based on specific values contained within Properties. Navigators can be used against a specific View, used in conjunction with either a query, a filter, or both. Navigators can be simple or very powerful, but provide a very useful way to capture results in logical buckets.

In [18]:
import pandas as pd  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell
from refinitiv.data.content import search  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell
import refinitiv.data as rd  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell

rd.open_session()  # rd.open_session() starts an authenticated connection to Refinitiv/LSEG (needed before queries)
#one example 
response = search.Definition(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'response' so we can reuse it later
    top=0,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'top' for the function call
    navigators="TransactionStatus"  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'navigators' for the function call
).get_data() 
rd.close_session()

response.data.raw['Navigators']['TransactionStatus']
Out[18]:

import pandas as pd  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell

#another one
response = search.Definition(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'response' so we can reuse it later
    top=0,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'top' for the function call
    navigators="TargetCountry"  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'navigators' for the function call
).get_data() 

rd.close_session()

response.data.raw['Navigators']['TargetCountry']

In [ ]:
import pandas as pd  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell
from refinitiv.data.content import search  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell
import refinitiv.data as rd  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell

rd.open_session()  # rd.open_session() starts an authenticated connection to Refinitiv/LSEG (needed before queries)
#another one
response = search.Definition(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'response' so we can reuse it later
    top=0,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'top' for the function call
    navigators="TargetPublicStatus"  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'navigators' for the function call
).get_data() 
rd.close_session()


response.data.raw['Navigators']['TargetPublicStatus']
Out[ ]:
{'Buckets': [{'Label': 'Private', 'Count': 769617},
  {'Label': 'Subsidiary', 'Count': 535202},
  {'Label': 'Public', 'Count': 260175},
  {'Label': 'Joint Venture', 'Count': 24856},
  {'Label': 'Government', 'Count': 12064},

The cell below searches for completed M&A deals where the target is a French private company, the deal value (including net debt) is at least 50 million, the form of the transaction is a merger or acquisition, and the announcement date falls between 2020 and 2025. Results are sorted by announcement date (most recent first).

A complete guide on how to set up filters and use the VIEWS in Refinitiv with Python can be found here.

In [ ]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)
import pandas as pd  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

rd.open_session()  # rd.open_session() starts an authenticated connection to Refinitiv/LSEG (needed before queries)

# Search for French private-target M&A deals, completed, value >= 50M, 2020–2025.
MnA = rd.discovery.search(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'MnA' so we can reuse it later
    view=rd.discovery.Views.DEALS_MERGERS_AND_ACQUISITIONS,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'view' for the function call
    filter=(  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'filter' for the function call
        "(TargetCountry eq 'FR' " 
        "and TransactionValueIncludingNetDebtOfTarget ge 50 " 
        "and TargetPublicStatus eq 'Private' " 
        "and TransactionStatus eq 'Completed' " 
        "and (FormOfTransactionName xeq 'Merger' " 
        "     or FormOfTransactionName xeq 'Acquisition') " 
        "and TransactionAnnouncementDate le 2025-12-31 " 
        "and TransactionAnnouncementDate ge 2020-01-01)" 
    ), 
    select=(  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'select' for the function call
        "TransactionAnnouncementDate, TargetCompanyName, TargetRIC, " 
        "TargetCountry, TargetPublicStatus, AcquirerCompanyName, " 
        "TransactionStatus, TransactionValueIncludingNetDebtOfTarget, " 
        "FormOfTransactionName" 
    ), 
    order_by="TransactionAnnouncementDate desc",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'order_by' for the function call
    top=1000  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'top' for the function call
) 

# Save to CSV and display the first rows.
MnA.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\ma_deals_FR.csv", index=True)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk
MnA.head(10)
Out[ ]:
TransactionAnnouncementDate TargetCompanyName TargetCountry TargetPublicStatus AcquirerCompanyName TransactionStatus TransactionValueIncludingNetDebtOfTarget FormOfTransactionName
0 2025-10-22 ImCheck Therapeutics SAS FR Private Ipsen SA Completed 405.93 Merger
1 2025-02-20 Asc3 Lmb Topco Sas FR Private Loar Group Inc Completed 380.366 Merger
2 2025-02-13 Stilla Technologies SAS FR Private Bio-Rad Laboratories Inc Completed 274.998 Merger
3 2024-10-07 Younited SA FR Private RA Special Acquisition Corp Completed 219.52 Merger
4 2024-09-25 Financiere Hmy Sas FR Private Itab Shop Concept AB Completed 357.76 Merger
5 2024-06-24 Preligens SAS FR Private Safran SA Completed 236.564 Merger
6 2023-06-16 Arverne Group SAS FR Private Transition SA Completed 181.687 Merger
7 2023-05-05 Wargraphs SAS FR Private MOBA Network AB Completed 55.055 Merger

9 2022-07-28 ATALIAN International SAS FR Private Clayton Dubilier & Rice LLC Completed 768.211 Merger

2.14. Extracting private equity deal information¶

Refinitiv also maintains a Private Equity and Venture Capital deals database. Access is via the INVESTORS Search view and the M&A deals view. The exact field names and filter values are documented in the Private Equity data guide and can be explored via DIB.

Navigators are a useful feature of the LSEG Search API: they return the distinct categorical values for a given field (similar to a "group by" in SQL). This lets you discover, for example, all possible values of InvestorType or InvestorAddressCountry before writing a filter.

The first cell below retrieves navigator buckets for the INVESTORS view. The second cell searches for French private equity investors. The third cell retrieves M&A deals for a specific PE firm.

In [61]:
import refinitiv.data as rd  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell
from refinitiv.data.content import search  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell
import pandas as pd  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell

rd.open_session()  # rd.open_session() starts an authenticated connection to Refinitiv/LSEG (needed before queries)

# Retrieve metadata for the INVESTORS view.
meta = search.metadata.Definition(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'meta' so we can reuse it later
    view=search.Views.INVESTORS  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'view' for the function call
).get_data() 
meta.data.df.to_csv("investors_metadata.csv", index=True)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk
print(meta.data.df[["Type", "Searchable", "Exact"]].to_string())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

rd.close_session() 

                              Type  Searchable  Exact
BusinessEntity              String        True   True
DocumentTitle               String        True  False
DTCharacteristics           String       False  False
DTSimpleType                String        True   True
DTSubjectName               String        True  False
HKAlias                     String        True   True
HKVersion                   String        True  False
InvestorAddressCity         String        True   True
InvestorAddressCountry      String        True   True
InvestorContactName         String        True  False
InvestorContactPermID       String        True   True
InvestorEntityType          String        True   True
InvestorEquityAssets        Double        True  False
InvestorFundParentName      String        True   True
InvestorFundParentOAPermID  String        True   True
InvestorId                  String        True  False
InvestorInvestmentStyle     String        True   True
InvestorLatestFilingDate      Date        True  False
InvestorName                String        True   True
InvestorOrientation         String        True   True
InvestorSubType             String        True   True
InvestorTurnover            String        True   True
InvestorType                String        True   True
PermID                      String        True   True
PI                          String        True  False
RIC                         String        True   True
SearchAllCategoryv3         String        True   True
Source                      String        True  False
SubjectBoost                String        True  False

The next cell uses navigators to see the distinct values for InvestorType, InvestorSubType, InvestorEntityType, and InvestorAddressCountry. Setting top=100 returns 100 document hits alongside the navigator buckets.

In [62]:
import refinitiv.data as rd  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell
from refinitiv.data.content import search  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell

rd.open_session()  # rd.open_session() starts an authenticated connection to Refinitiv/LSEG (needed before queries)
# navigator to get all the possible values for InvestorSubType
response = search.Definition(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'response' so we can reuse it later
    top=0,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'top' for the function call
    navigators="InvestorSubType"  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'navigators' for the function call
).get_data() 
rd.close_session() 

response.data.raw['Navigators']['InvestorSubType']

# navigator to get all the possible values for InvestorAddressCountry

    top=0,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'top' for the function call
    navigators="InvestorAddressCountry"  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'navigators' for the function call
).get_data() 
rd.close_session()

The next cell searches for all French private equity investors, and the one after retrieves M&A deals for a specific PE firm.

In [64]:
import refinitiv.data as rd  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell
import pandas as pd  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell

rd.open_session()  # rd.open_session() starts an authenticated connection to Refinitiv/LSEG (needed before queries)

french_pe_investors = rd.discovery.search(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'french_pe_investors' so we can reuse it later
    view=rd.discovery.Views.INVESTORS,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'view' for the function call
    filter="InvestorSubType eq 'Private Equity' and InvestorAddressCountry eq 'France'",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'filter' for the function call
    select="InvestorName, InvestorSubType",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'select' for the function call
    top=200  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'top' for the function call
) 

rd.close_session() 

print(f"Found {len(french_pe_investors)} private equity investors in France:\n")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print(french_pe_investors.to_string(index=False))  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

The next cell retrieves M&A deals for one specific PE firm (BNP Paribas Private Equity).

In [65]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell
import pandas as pd  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell

rd.open_session()  # rd.open_session() starts an authenticated connection to Refinitiv/LSEG (needed before queries)

fund_name = "BNP Paribas Private Equity"  # variable assignment: store a value in 'fund_name' so we can reuse it later

deals = rd.discovery.search(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'deals' so we can reuse it later
    view=rd.discovery.Views.DEALS_MERGERS_AND_ACQUISITIONS,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'view' for the function call
    filter=f"AcquirerCompanyName eq '{fund_name}'",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'filter' for the function call
    select=(  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'select' for the function call
        "TransactionAnnouncementDate, AcquirerCompanyName, AcquirerCountry, " 
        "TargetCompanyName, TargetCountry, TargetPublicStatus, " 
        "TransactionStatus, TransactionValueIncludingNetDebtOfTarget, " 
        "FormOfTransactionName, PercentageOfSharesAcquired, SdcDealNumber" 
    ), 
    order_by="TransactionAnnouncementDate desc",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'order_by' for the function call
    top=500  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'top' for the function call
) 

print(f"Found {len(deals)} deals for {fund_name}")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print(deals.to_string())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

Found 6 deals for BNP Paribas Private Equity
  TransactionAnnouncementDate                           AcquirerCompanyName AcquirerCountry         TargetCompanyName TargetCountry TargetPublicStatus TransactionStatus  TransactionValueIncludingNetDebtOfTarget            FormOfTransactionName  PercentageOfSharesAcquired SdcDealNumber

1                  2021-07-23  BNP Paribas Fortis Private Equity Belgium NV              BL                Konings NV            BL            Private         Completed                                       0.0  Acquisition Of Partial Interest                           0    3782428040
2                  2018-01-01  BNP Paribas Fortis Private Equity Belgium NV              BL  Food Associates Group NV            BL            Private         Completed                                       0.0  Acquisition Of Partial Interest                          25    3327525040
3                  2010-06-21                BNP Paribas Private Equity SAS              FR      Acropolis Telecom SA            FR             Public         Completed                                     2.239  Acquisition Of Partial Interest                           0    2194202040
4                  2010-05-31                BNP Paribas Private Equity SAS              FR                Telogic AS            DN            Private         Completed                                     3.691  Acquisition Of Partial Interest                           0    2207613040

2.15. Extracting director and CEO information¶

Refinitiv provides officer and director data for listed companies: names, titles, ages, gender, and biographies. The workflow is:

  1. Search for the company's equity RIC using the EQUITY_QUOTES view.
  2. Use rd.get_data() with officer fields (e.g. TR.OfficerName, TR.OfficerTitle, TR.OfficerAge, TR.ODOfficerPersonGender, TR.ODOfficerBiography).
  3. Control how many officers are returned via ranking parameters (Rnk for officer fields, ODRnk for OD-prefixed fields).

The example below retrieves board and management information for Dassault.

In [66]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

rd.open_session()  # rd.open_session() starts an authenticated connection to Refinitiv/LSEG (needed before queries)

# ── STEP 1: Find Dassault equity RIC ─────────────────────────────────────────
equity_hits = rd.discovery.search(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'equity_hits' so we can reuse it later
    query="Dassault",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'query' for the function call
    view=rd.discovery.Views.EQUITY_QUOTES,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'view' for the function call
    top=20  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'top' for the function call
) 

print("=== Candidate Dassault equities ===")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print(equity_hits[["RIC", "DocumentTitle"]].to_string(index=False))  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
equity_hits.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\dassault_equity_candidates.csv", index=False)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk

# Take the first candidate (replace with the specific RIC you want).
selected_ric = equity_hits.iloc[0]["RIC"]  # variable assignment: store a value in 'selected_ric' so we can reuse it later
print(f"\nSelected RIC: {selected_ric}")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# ── STEP 2: Retrieve officer / director details ──────────────────────────────
officers = rd.get_data(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'officers' so we can reuse it later
    universe=[selected_ric],  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=[  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
        "TR.OfficerName", 
        "TR.OfficerTitle", 
        "TR.OfficerAge", 
        "TR.ODOfficerPersonGender", 
        "TR.ODOfficerBiography" 
    ], 
    parameters={"Rnk": "R1:R20", "ODRnk": "R1:R20"},  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'parameters' for the function call
    use_field_names_in_headers=True  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'use_field_names_in_headers' for the function call
) 

print("\n=== Officer / director panel ===")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print(officers)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
officers.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\dassault_officers_raw.csv", index=True)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk

rd.close_session()

2.16. Extracting news flow for a company¶

Refinitiv provides access to news headlines and full stories for listed companies. News data can be used to study information arrival, sentiment, or to link specific events to stock price reactions.

The workflow has two steps:

  1. rd.news.get_headlines() — Retrieves a table of headlines for a given RIC and date range. The query parameter uses Refinitiv query syntax: "R:NVDA.O" targets the NVIDIA RIC, and "Language:LEN" restricts to English-language news.
  2. rd.news.get_story() — Retrieves the full text of a specific news story, identified by its storyId from the headlines table.

For full documentation, call help(rd.news.get_headlines) and help(rd.news.get_story), or see the official LSEG reference.

In [ ]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)

# Display documentation for the headline retrieval function.
help(rd.news.get_headlines) 

Help on function get_headlines in module refinitiv.data._access_layer.news._news:

get_headlines(query: str, count: int = 10, start: 'OptDateTime' = None, end: 'OptDateTime' = None, order_by: Union[str, refinitiv.data.content.news.headlines._sort_order.SortOrder] = <SortOrder.new_to_old: 'newToOld'>) -> pandas.core.frame.DataFrame
    Retrieves news headlines.

    Parameters
    ----------
    query: str
        The user search query for news headlines.
    count: int, optional
        Count to limit number of headlines.
    start: str or timedelta, optional
        Beginning of date range.
        String format is: '%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S'. e.g. '2016-01-20T15:04:05'.
    end: str or timedelta, optional
        End of date range.
        String format is: '%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S'. e.g. '2016-01-20T15:04:05'.
    order_by: str or SortOrder
        Sort order for headline items.

    Returns
    -------
    pd.DataFrame
        Headlines dataframe

    Examples
    --------
    >>> from datetime import timedelta
    >>> import refinitiv.data as rd
    >>> response = rd.news.get_headlines(
    ...     "Refinitiv",
    ...     start="20.03.2021",
    ...     end=timedelta(days=-4),
    ...     count=3
    ... )
In [68]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
rd.open_session()  # rd.open_session() starts an authenticated connection to Refinitiv/LSEG (needed before queries)

ric = "NVDA.O"   # NVIDIA

# ── STEP 1: Get headlines ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# "R:{ric}" targets the specific instrument; "Language:LEN" = English only.
headlines = rd.news.get_headlines(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'headlines' so we can reuse it later
    query=f"R:{ric} AND Language:LEN",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'query' for the function call
    start="2024-01-01",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'start' for the function call
    end="2024-12-31",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'end' for the function call
    count=100               # maximum headlines per call
) 

headlines = headlines.reset_index()   # versionCreated becomes a regular column
print(f"=== {len(headlines)} headlines for {ric} ===")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print(headlines[["versionCreated", "headline", "storyId"]].to_string())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

99 2024-12-30 15:06:51.018                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             Analyzing NVIDIA In Comparison To Competitors In Semiconductors &amp; Semiconductor Equipment Industry     urn:newsml:newsroom:20241230:nNRAuy8rzw:0

The next cell loops through each headline retrieved above, fetches the full story text using rd.news.get_story(), and saves everything to an Excel file. We add a 0.2-second pause between calls to avoid hitting the Refinitiv API rate limit. This cell depends on the headlines DataFrame created just above.

Before retrieving full story text, let us inspect the documentation of rd.news.get_story().

In [70]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)

# Display documentation for the headline retrieval function.
help(rd.news.get_story) 

Help on function get_story in module refinitiv.data._access_layer.news._news:

get_story(story_id: str, format: Union[refinitiv.data._access_layer.news._news.Format, str] = <Format.HTML: 'Html'>) -> str
    Retrieves the news story items.

    Parameters
    ----------
    story_id : str
        News Story ID.
    format : str, Format, optional
        Response format.

    Returns
    -------
    str
        Story html or text response

    Examples
    --------
    >>> import refinitiv.data as rd
    >>> response = rd.news.get_story("urn:newsml:reuters.com:20220713:nL1N2YU10J", format=rd.news.Format.TEXT)
In [74]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import pandas as pd  # pandas is the standard Python library for working with tabular data (DataFrames)
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
from datetime import datetime, date  # datetime provides date/time objects and parsing
import time

rd.open_session()  # open a session so we can request data from the platform

# ── STEP 2: Get full story text for each headline ────────────────────────────
# This cell depends on the 'headlines' DataFrame from the cell above.
# Take times to run, refer to xlsx file!

results = []  # variable assignment: store a value in 'results' so we can reuse it later

for _, row in headlines.iterrows():  # for-loop: repeat code for each element in an iterable
    # Retrieve the full text of each story by its storyId.
    story_text = rd.news.get_story(row["storyId"], format=rd.news.Format.TEXT)  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'story_text' for the function call
    results.append({ 
        "date":     row["versionCreated"], 
        "headline": row["headline"], 
        "storyId":  row["storyId"], 
        "text":     story_text 
    }) 
    time.sleep(0.2)   # pause 0.2 seconds between calls to avoid rate limiting

df_news = pd.DataFrame(results)  # variable assignment: store a value in 'df_news' so we can reuse it later
print(df_news[["date", "headline"]].to_string())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# Save to Excel.
df_news.to_excel('news_NVDA.xlsx', index=False)  # to_excel() writes a DataFrame to an Excel (.xlsx) file

rd.close_session()

2.17. Extracting bond information and identifying green bonds¶

In this subsection we use the GOV_CORP_INSTRUMENTS Search view to build a small bond universe and then isolate green bonds — bonds whose proceeds are specifically earmarked for environmental or climate-related projects.

We search for bonds issued by TotalEnergies, inspect their characteristics (RIC, ISIN, maturity, coupon, currency, green-bond flag), and then retrieve a Yield to Maturity (YTM) time series for one green bond.

Yield to Maturity (YTM) is the annualised return you would earn if you bought the bond today and held it until maturity, collecting all coupons along the way. YTM moves inversely with price: when the bond's market price falls, YTM rises, and vice versa. Daily changes are mostly driven by macro interest rate moves (e.g. ECB rate expectations), shifts in the issuer's credit risk, and liquidity.

In [72]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell
from refinitiv.data.content import search  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell
import pandas as pd  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell

rd.open_session()  # rd.open_session() starts an authenticated connection to Refinitiv/LSEG (needed before queries)

# Retrieve metadata for the INVESTORS view.
meta = search.metadata.Definition(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'meta' so we can reuse it later
    view=search.Views.GOV_CORP_INSTRUMENTS  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'view' for the function call
).get_data() 
meta.data.df.to_csv("bond_metadata.csv", index=True)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk
print(meta.data.df[["Type", "Searchable", "Exact"]].to_string())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

rd.close_session()

# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)
import pandas as pd  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell
from refinitiv.data.content import search  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

rd.open_session()  # rd.open_session() starts an authenticated connection to Refinitiv/LSEG (needed before queries)

# Search for TotalEnergies bonds in the GOV_CORP_INSTRUMENTS view.
response_total = search.Definition(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'response_total' so we can reuse it later
    view=search.Views.GOV_CORP_INSTRUMENTS,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'view' for the function call
    query="TotalEnergies",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'query' for the function call
    select="RIC, ISIN, MaturityDate, CouponRate, Currency, IsGreenBond",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'select' for the function call
    top=100  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'top' for the function call
).get_data() 

total_bonds = response_total.data.df  # variable assignment: store a value in 'total_bonds' so we can reuse it later

print("=== Bonds for TotalEnergies (up to 100) ===")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print(total_bonds.to_string())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# Save to CSV.
total_bonds.to_csv(  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk
    BASE_FOLDER + "\\total_energies_bonds_gov_corp_instruments.csv", index=False 
) 

=== Bonds for TotalEnergies (up to 100) ===

The next cell retrieves a daily Yield to Maturity time series for one of the bond found in the table above.

In [76]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import pandas as pd  # pandas is the standard Python library for working with tabular data (DataFrames)
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
from datetime import datetime, date  # datetime provides date/time objects and parsing
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

rd.open_session()  # rd.open_session() starts an authenticated connection to Refinitiv/LSEG (needed before queries)

# Retrieve daily Yield to Maturity for one green bond over 2023–2024.
# Replace 'first_green_ric' with the actual RIC from the bond table above.
bond_yield_ts = rd.get_data(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'bond_yield_ts' so we can reuse it later
    universe="FR200438124=",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=["TR.YieldToMaturity", "TR.YieldToMaturity.date"],  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
    parameters={"SDate": "2023-01-01", "EDate": "2024-12-31", "Frq": "D"}  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'parameters' for the function call
) 

print(bond_yield_ts.head())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# Rename columns, convert dates, and compute daily yield changes.
bond_yield_ts = bond_yield_ts.rename(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'bond_yield_ts' so we can reuse it later
    columns={"Yield to Maturity": "YIELD", "Date": "Date"}  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'columns' for the function call
) 
bond_yield_ts["Date"] = pd.to_datetime(bond_yield_ts["Date"])  # pd.to_datetime() converts text/dates into pandas datetime objects
bond_yield_ts = bond_yield_ts.set_index("Date").sort_index()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'bond_yield_ts' so we can reuse it later
bond_yield_ts["YIELD_CHANGE"] = bond_yield_ts["YIELD"].diff() 

print(bond_yield_ts.head())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
bond_yield_ts.to_csv(  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk
    BASE_FOLDER + "\\total_energies_first_green_bond_yield.csv" 
) 

rd.close_session() 

     Instrument  Yield to Maturity       Date
0  FR200438124=               3.77 2023-01-02
1  FR200438124=               3.68 2023-01-03
2  FR200438124=               3.57 2023-01-04
3  FR200438124=                3.6 2023-01-05
4  FR200438124=               3.49 2023-01-06
              Instrument  YIELD  YIELD_CHANGE
Date                                         
2023-01-02  FR200438124=   3.77          <NA>
2023-01-03  FR200438124=   3.68         -0.09
2023-01-04  FR200438124=   3.57         -0.11
2023-01-05  FR200438124=    3.6          0.03
2023-01-06  FR200438124=   3.49         -0.11

2.18. Merging collected data¶

You will typically download several datasets and need to merge them into one to perform some data analysis. Below we show how to merge dataset based either on time or RIC. The key function here is merge().

In [77]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import pandas as pd  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell
help(pd.merge) 

Help on function merge in module pandas.core.reshape.merge:

merge(left: 'DataFrame | Series', right: 'DataFrame | Series', how: 'MergeHow' = 'inner', on: 'IndexLabel | AnyArrayLike | None' = None, left_on: 'IndexLabel | AnyArrayLike | None' = None, right_on: 'IndexLabel | AnyArrayLike | None' = None, left_index: 'bool' = False, right_index: 'bool' = False, sort: 'bool' = False, suffixes: 'Suffixes' = ('_x', '_y'), copy: 'bool | None' = None, indicator: 'str | bool' = False, validate: 'str | None' = None) -> 'DataFrame'
    Merge DataFrame or named Series objects with a database-style join.

    A named Series object is treated as a DataFrame with a single named column.

    The join is done on columns or indexes. If joining columns on
    columns, the DataFrame indexes *will be ignored*. Otherwise if joining indexes
    on indexes or indexes on a column or columns, the index will be passed on.
    When performing a cross merge, no column specifications to merge on are
    allowed.

    .. warning::

        If both key columns contain rows where the key is a null value, those
        rows will be matched against each other. This is different from usual SQL
        join behaviour and can lead to unexpected results.

    Parameters
    ----------
    left : DataFrame or named Series
    right : DataFrame or named Series
        Object to merge with.
    how : {'left', 'right', 'outer', 'inner', 'cross'}, default 'inner'
        Type of merge to be performed.

        * left: use only keys from left frame, similar to a SQL left outer join;
          preserve key order.
        * right: use only keys from right frame, similar to a SQL right outer join;
          preserve key order.
        * outer: use union of keys from both frames, similar to a SQL full outer
          join; sort keys lexicographically.
        * inner: use intersection of keys from both frames, similar to a SQL inner
          join; preserve the order of the left keys.
        * cross: creates the cartesian product from both frames, preserves the order
          of the left keys.
    on : label or list
        Column or index level names to join on. These must be found in both
        DataFrames. If `on` is None and not merging on indexes then this defaults
        to the intersection of the columns in both DataFrames.
    left_on : label or list, or array-like
        Column or index level names to join on in the left DataFrame. Can also
        be an array or list of arrays of the length of the left DataFrame.
        These arrays are treated as if they are columns.
    right_on : label or list, or array-like
        Column or index level names to join on in the right DataFrame. Can also
        be an array or list of arrays of the length of the right DataFrame.
        These arrays are treated as if they are columns.
    left_index : bool, default False
        Use the index from the left DataFrame as the join key(s). If it is a
        MultiIndex, the number of keys in the other DataFrame (either the index
        or a number of columns) must match the number of levels.
    right_index : bool, default False
        Use the index from the right DataFrame as the join key. Same caveats as
        left_index.
    sort : bool, default False
        Sort the join keys lexicographically in the result DataFrame. If False,
        the order of the join keys depends on the join type (how keyword).
    suffixes : list-like, default is ("_x", "_y")
        A length-2 sequence where each element is optionally a string
        indicating the suffix to add to overlapping column names in
        `left` and `right` respectively. Pass a value of `None` instead
        of a string to indicate that the column name from `left` or
        `right` should be left as-is, with no suffix. At least one of the
        values must not be None.
    copy : bool, default True
        If False, avoid copy if possible.

        .. note::
            The `copy` keyword will change behavior in pandas 3.0.
            `Copy-on-Write
            <https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/user_guide/copy_on_write.html>`__
            will be enabled by default, which means that all methods with a
            `copy` keyword will use a lazy copy mechanism to defer the copy and
            ignore the `copy` keyword. The `copy` keyword will be removed in a
            future version of pandas.

            You can already get the future behavior and improvements through
            enabling copy on write ``pd.options.mode.copy_on_write = True``
    indicator : bool or str, default False
        If True, adds a column to the output DataFrame called "_merge" with
        information on the source of each row. The column can be given a different
        name by providing a string argument. The column will have a Categorical
        type with the value of "left_only" for observations whose merge key only
        appears in the left DataFrame, "right_only" for observations
        whose merge key only appears in the right DataFrame, and "both"
        if the observation's merge key is found in both DataFrames.

    validate : str, optional
        If specified, checks if merge is of specified type.

        * "one_to_one" or "1:1": check if merge keys are unique in both
          left and right datasets.
        * "one_to_many" or "1:m": check if merge keys are unique in left
          dataset.
        * "many_to_one" or "m:1": check if merge keys are unique in right
          dataset.
        * "many_to_many" or "m:m": allowed, but does not result in checks.

    Returns
    -------
    DataFrame
        A DataFrame of the two merged objects.

    See Also
    --------
    merge_ordered : Merge with optional filling/interpolation.
    merge_asof : Merge on nearest keys.
    DataFrame.join : Similar method using indices.

    Examples
    --------
    >>> df1 = pd.DataFrame({'lkey': ['foo', 'bar', 'baz', 'foo'],
    ...                     'value': [1, 2, 3, 5]})
    >>> df2 = pd.DataFrame({'rkey': ['foo', 'bar', 'baz', 'foo'],
    ...                     'value': [5, 6, 7, 8]})
    >>> df1
        lkey value
    0   foo      1
    1   bar      2
    2   baz      3
    3   foo      5
    >>> df2
        rkey value
    0   foo      5
    1   bar      6
    2   baz      7
    3   foo      8

    Merge df1 and df2 on the lkey and rkey columns. The value columns have
    the default suffixes, _x and _y, appended.

    >>> df1.merge(df2, left_on='lkey', right_on='rkey')
      lkey  value_x rkey  value_y
    0  foo        1  foo        5
    1  foo        1  foo        8
    2  bar        2  bar        6
    3  baz        3  baz        7
    4  foo        5  foo        5
    5  foo        5  foo        8

    Merge DataFrames df1 and df2 with specified left and right suffixes
    appended to any overlapping columns.

    >>> df1.merge(df2, left_on='lkey', right_on='rkey',
    ...           suffixes=('_left', '_right'))
      lkey  value_left rkey  value_right
    0  foo           1  foo            5
    1  foo           1  foo            8
    2  bar           2  bar            6
    3  baz           3  baz            7
    4  foo           5  foo            5
    5  foo           5  foo            8

    Merge DataFrames df1 and df2, but raise an exception if the DataFrames have
    any overlapping columns.

    >>> df1.merge(df2, left_on='lkey', right_on='rkey', suffixes=(False, False))
    Traceback (most recent call last):
    ...
    ValueError: columns overlap but no suffix specified:
        Index(['value'], dtype='object')

    >>> df1 = pd.DataFrame({'a': ['foo', 'bar'], 'b': [1, 2]})
    >>> df2 = pd.DataFrame({'a': ['foo', 'baz'], 'c': [3, 4]})
    >>> df1
          a  b
    0   foo  1
    1   bar  2
    >>> df2
          a  c
    0   foo  3
    1   baz  4

    >>> df1.merge(df2, how='inner', on='a')
          a  b  c
    0   foo  1  3

    >>> df1.merge(df2, how='left', on='a')
          a  b  c
    0   foo  1  3.0
    1   bar  2  NaN

    >>> df1 = pd.DataFrame({'left': ['foo', 'bar']})
    >>> df2 = pd.DataFrame({'right': [7, 8]})
    >>> df1
        left
    0   foo
    1   bar
    >>> df2
        right
    0   7
    1   8

    >>> df1.merge(df2, how='cross')
       left  right
    0   foo      7
    1   foo      8
    2   bar      7
    3   bar      8
In [78]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import pandas as pd  # pandas is the standard Python library for working with tabular data (DataFrames)
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

rd.open_session()  # rd.open_session() starts an authenticated connection to Refinitiv/LSEG (needed before queries)

# Get constituent
constituents = pd.read_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\cac40_constituents.csv")  # variable assignment: store a value in 'constituents' so we can reuse it later
rics = constituents["RIC"].tolist()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'rics' so we can reuse it later

# collect industry information
ind = rd.get_data(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'ind' so we can reuse it later
    universe=rics,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=["TR.GICSSector"],  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
    use_field_names_in_headers=True,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'use_field_names_in_headers' for the function call
) 
print("="*40)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print(ind.head())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# collect country information
count = rd.get_data(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'count' so we can reuse it later
    universe=rics,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=["TR.HeadquartersCountry"],  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
    use_field_names_in_headers=True,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'use_field_names_in_headers' for the function call
) 
print("="*40)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print(count.head())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# collect name information
names = rd.get_data(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'names' so we can reuse it later
    universe=rics,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=["TR.CommonName"],  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
    use_field_names_in_headers=True,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'use_field_names_in_headers' for the function call
) 
print("="*40)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print(names.head())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

#close session
rd.close_session() 

# merge name, country, industry
ind_country=ind.merge(count, on="Instrument")  # variable assignment: store a value in 'ind_country' so we can reuse it later
ind_country_name=ind_country.merge(names, on="Instrument")  # variable assignment: store a value in 'ind_country_name' so we can reuse it later
print("="*40)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print(ind_country_name.head())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# save
ind_country_name.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\merged_dataset.csv", index=True)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk

1    DAST.PA  Information Technology
2    FOUG.PA             Industrials

4    ORAN.PA  Communication Services

0    SOGN.PA              Financials                 France
