6. Regression Analysis¶

6.1. What is a univariate regression?¶

A univariate (simple) regression models the linear relationship between one dependent variable Y (what you want to explain) and one independent variable X (what you think explains Y):

$$Y = \alpha + \beta X + \varepsilon$$

  • $\alpha$ (intercept): the predicted value of Y when X = 0.
  • $\beta$ (slope): how much Y changes for a one-unit increase in X. This is the key number.
  • $\varepsilon$ (residual): the part of Y that the model cannot explain — random noise.

OLS (Ordinary Least Squares) finds the line that minimises the sum of squared residuals — the vertical distances between each data point and the fitted line.

Key regression outputs:

Output Meaning Rule of thumb
$\beta$ (slope) Effect of X on Y Sign = direction, size = magnitude
t-statistic Is $\beta$ significantly different from 0? |t| > 2 → likely significant
p-value Probability of observing this $\beta$ if true $\beta$ = 0 p < 0.05 → significant
R² Fraction of Y's variance explained by X 0 = nothing explained, 1 = perfect
95% CI Range where the true $\beta$ likely falls If it excludes 0 → significant

How OLS finds the regression line : The residual for observation $i$ is $e_i = Y_i - (\alpha + \beta X_i)$. OLS picks the $\alpha$ and $\beta$ that minimise the Sum of Squared Residuals:

$$SSR = \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i^2$$

Why squared? So that positive and negative errors do not cancel out, and large errors are penalised more heavily.

6.2. Application — Regression of ROA on leverage¶

statsmodels — a Python package for statistical models. statsmodels is the standard Python library for estimating regression models and running statistical tests. The module we import is statsmodels.api (aliased as sm). The main tools we use are:

  • sm.OLS(y, X) — creates an Ordinary Least Squares regression object.
  • .fit() — estimates the model parameters.
  • .summary() — prints a detailed output table (coefficients, standard errors, t-statistics, p-values, R²).
  • sm.add_constant(X) — adds a column of 1s to the regressor matrix so that the model includes an intercept ($\\alpha$).

We import it with: import statsmodels.api as sm

statsmodels documentation: https://www.statsmodels.org/stable/index.html

We now test one of the most studied relationships in corporate finance: does higher leverage (debt) lead to lower profitability (ROA)?

Three competing theories make different predictions:

  1. Static trade-off theory: Modigliani & Miller (1963) showed that interest tax shields make debt valuable. Later work (Kraus & Litzenberger, 1973) added that excessive debt also raises financial distress costs. Balancing the two forces predicts an optimal debt level — an inverted-U relationship between leverage and firm value.
  2. Agency theory (Jensen, 1986): Debt disciplines managers, so more leverage could improve performance. Predicts a positive relationship.
  3. Pecking order theory (Myers & Majluf, 1984): Firms borrow only when they are unable to fund investments internally — i.e. when profits are low. Predicts a negative relationship.

Most empirical studies on large US firms (e.g. Fama & French 1992, Frank & Goyal 2009) find a negative relationship, consistent with pecking order theory.

Our hypotheses: H₀: $\beta$ = 0 (leverage has no effect on ROA). H₁: $\beta$ < 0 (higher leverage → lower ROA). We test hypothesis in a similar way as for t-test, we look a the p-value attached to the coefficient of the regression, assess its statistical significance (usually at 5%) and conclude on whether we reject or not the null hypothesis (H₀).

In [147]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import pandas as pd  # pandas is the standard Python library for working with tabular data (DataFrames)
import statsmodels.api as sm  # statsmodels provides statistical models (e.g., OLS regression)
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
rd.open_session()  # open a session so we can request data from the platform

# ── 1. Load S&P 500 constituents from the CSV saved in Section 3.2 ───────────
sp500_constituents = pd.read_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\sp500_constituents_trbc.csv")  # variable assignment: store a value in 'sp500_constituents' so we can reuse it later
rics_sp500 = sp500_constituents["TR.RIC"].astype(str).tolist()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'rics_sp500' so we can reuse it later

# ── 2. Download accounting data ──────────────────────────────────────────────
rd.open_session()  # rd.open_session() starts an authenticated connection to Refinitiv/LSEG (needed before queries)
fundamentals = rd.get_data(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'fundamentals' so we can reuse it later
    universe=rics_sp500,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=["TR.NETINCOME", "TR.TOTALASSETS", "TR.TOTALDEBT"],  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
    use_field_names_in_headers=True,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'use_field_names_in_headers' for the function call
) 
rd.close_session() 

# ── 3. Build ROA and leverage ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
df = fundamentals[["TR.NETINCOME", "TR.TOTALASSETS", "TR.TOTALDEBT"]].copy()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'df' so we can reuse it later
df = df.rename(columns={  # variable assignment: store a value in 'df' so we can reuse it later
    "TR.NETINCOME":   "net_income", 
    "TR.TOTALASSETS":  "total_assets", 
    "TR.TOTALDEBT":    "total_debt", 
}) 
df = df.loc[df["total_assets"] > 0].copy()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'df' so we can reuse it later
df["roa"]      = df["net_income"] / df["total_assets"] 
df["leverage"] = df["total_debt"] / df["total_assets"] 
df = df[["roa", "leverage"]].dropna()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'df' so we can reuse it later

print(f"Usable observations: {len(df)}")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# ── 4. OLS regression: ROA ~ leverage ─────────────────────────────────────────
y = df["roa"].astype(float)  # variable assignment: store a value in 'y' so we can reuse it later
X = sm.add_constant(df["leverage"].astype(float))  # variable assignment: store a value in 'X' so we can reuse it later
model = sm.OLS(y, X).fit()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'model' so we can reuse it later
print(model.summary())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

Usable observations: 435
                            OLS Regression Results                            
==============================================================================
Dep. Variable:                    roa   R-squared:                       0.013
Model:                            OLS   Adj. R-squared:                  0.010
Method:                 Least Squares   F-statistic:                     5.568
Date:                Sat, 28 Feb 2026   Prob (F-statistic):             0.0187
Time:                        18:56:49   Log-Likelihood:                 488.64
No. Observations:                 435   AIC:                            -973.3
Df Residuals:                     433   BIC:                            -965.1
Df Model:                           1                                         
Covariance Type:            nonrobust                                         
==============================================================================
                 coef    std err          t      P>|t|      [0.025      0.975]
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
const          0.0579      0.007      8.737      0.000       0.045       0.071
leverage       0.0422      0.018      2.360      0.019       0.007       0.077
==============================================================================
Omnibus:                      105.974   Durbin-Watson:                   1.974
Prob(Omnibus):                  0.000   Jarque-Bera (JB):             2231.863
Skew:                           0.427   Prob(JB):                         0.00
Kurtosis:                      14.064   Cond. No.                         5.19
==============================================================================

Notes:
[1] Standard Errors assume that the covariance matrix of the errors is correctly specified.

Statistical interpretation: The slope $\beta$ on leverage is significantly positive (p-value=0.021), consistent with the agency theory (Jensen, 1986). Note that this sign is the opposite of the negative relationship commonly reported in large US firm samples and of the pecking-order direction stated in our H₁. This illustrates an important point: empirical results depend on the sample, time period, and market studied — always interpret coefficients in context rather than assuming a single canonical sign.

Economic interpretation: Leverage is measured as a proportion (0 to 1), so the coefficient $\beta = 0.04$ means a one-unit increase in leverage (i.e. from 0% to 100% debt-to-assets) is associated with a 0.04 increase in ROA. Equivalently, a one percentage point increase in leverage (0.01 in ratio terms) is associated with a $0.04 \times 0.01 = 0.0004$ change in ROA (about 0.04 pp). To get a more intuitive sense of magnitude, we can scale by the typical variation across firms: a one standard deviation increase in leverage is associated with a $\beta \times \text{SD(leverage)}$ change in ROA — more informative than an arbitrary 1 pp change because it reflects how much leverage actually varies in the data.

In [148]:
df.describe() # df.describe() computes summary statistics for numeric columns
Out[148]:
roa leverage
count 435.0 435.0
mean 0.070719 0.304153
std 0.079286 0.211693
min -0.48786 0.0
25% 0.027858 0.162837
50% 0.058363 0.277042
75% 0.103205 0.420187
max 0.622747 1.63955

Economic interpretation: The standard deviation of leverage across S&P 500 firms is 0.211, so a one standard deviation increase in leverage is associated with a 0.04 × 0.211 = 0.0084 change in ROA (about 0.84 pp). Given that the standard deviation of ROA is 0.079, this represents 0.0084 / 0.079 ≈ 0.11 standard deviations of ROA — a small effect. A typical variation in leverage across firms is associated with less than a 1 percentage point shift in profitability, suggesting that leverage has some explanatory power but remains a relatively minor driver of ROA differences among S&P 500 firms.

R² (R-squared) measures the proportion of the variation in the dependent variable (e.g. ROA) that is explained by the independent variable(s) (e.g. leverage). It ranges from 0 to 1. An R² of 0.12 means leverage explains 12% of the variation in ROA across firms — the remaining 88% comes from other factors we haven't included. An R² of 0.90 would mean our model captures almost all the variation. We want this information because it tells us how useful the model is as a whole. A coefficient can be statistically significant (small p-value) yet explain very little of what's going on. R² gives that bigger picture: yes, the relationship exists, but how much does it actually matter in explaining the outcome?

6.3. Multivariate regression — two independent variables¶

In a univariate regression we asked: "does leverage explain ROA?" But ROA is driven by many factors simultaneously. A multivariate regression lets us test several explanatory variables at once:

$$ROA_i = \alpha + \beta_1 \cdot Leverage_i + \beta_2 \cdot ESG_i + \varepsilon_i$$

The key concept is "holding everything else constant":

  • $\beta_1$ = effect of leverage on ROA, holding ESG constant.
  • $\beta_2$ = effect of ESG on ROA, holding leverage constant.

This is powerful because it separates the effect of each variable. Without controlling for ESG, the leverage coefficient might be biased — a phenomenon called omitted variable bias. For example, if high-ESG firms also have low leverage and high ROA, then a model without ESG would incorrectly attribute the ESG effect to leverage.

Hypothesis on ESG: A meta-analysis of over 2,000 studies (Friede et al. 2015) finds that ESG is positively related to financial performance in about 63% of studies. We test H₀: $\beta_2$ = 0 vs H₁: $\beta_2$ > 0.

In [149]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import pandas as pd  # pandas is the standard Python library for working with tabular data (DataFrames)
import statsmodels.api as sm  # statsmodels provides statistical models (e.g., OLS regression)
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)

# ── 1. Download fundamentals + ESG ───────────────────────────────────────────
rd.open_session()  # rd.open_session() starts an authenticated connection to Refinitiv/LSEG (needed before queries)

fundamentals = rd.get_data(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'fundamentals' so we can reuse it later
    universe=rics_sp500,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=["TR.NETINCOME", "TR.TOTALASSETS", "TR.TOTALDEBT", "TR.ESGSCORE"],  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
    use_field_names_in_headers=True,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'use_field_names_in_headers' for the function call
) 

rd.close_session() 

# ── 2. Build variables ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
df = fundamentals.rename(columns={  # variable assignment: store a value in 'df' so we can reuse it later
    "TR.NETINCOME":   "net_income", 
    "TR.TOTALASSETS":  "total_assets", 
    "TR.TOTALDEBT":    "total_debt", 
    "TR.ESGSCORE":     "esg", 
}).copy() 

for col in ["net_income", "total_assets", "total_debt", "esg"]:  # for-loop: repeat code for each element in an iterable
    df[col] = pd.to_numeric(df[col], errors="coerce") 

df = df.loc[df["total_assets"] > 0].copy()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'df' so we can reuse it later
df["roa"]      = df["net_income"] / df["total_assets"] 
df["leverage"] = df["total_debt"] / df["total_assets"] 
df = df[["roa", "leverage", "esg"]].dropna().astype(float)  # variable assignment: store a value in 'df' so we can reuse it later

print(f"Usable observations: {len(df)}")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# ── 3. Fit models ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
m2    = sm.OLS(df["roa"], sm.add_constant(df[["leverage", "esg"]])).fit()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'm2' so we can reuse it later
print(m2.summary())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

                 coef    std err          t      P>|t|      [0.025      0.975]
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
const          0.0833      0.020      4.186      0.000       0.044       0.122
leverage       0.0422      0.018      2.335      0.020       0.007       0.078
esg           -0.0089      0.007     -1.315      0.189      -0.022       0.004
==============================================================================
Omnibus:                      102.972   Durbin-Watson:                   1.971
Prob(Omnibus):                  0.000   Jarque-Bera (JB):             2234.485
Skew:                           0.395   Prob(JB):                         0.00
Kurtosis:                      14.192   Cond. No.                         17.4

Notes:
[1] Standard Errors assume that the covariance matrix of the errors is correctly specified.
In [150]:
df.describe()  # df.describe() computes summary statistics for numeric columns
Out[150]:
roa leverage esg
count 426.000000 426.000000 426.000000
mean 0.071025 0.302753 2.815903
std 0.079228 0.211562 0.565560
min -0.487860 0.000000 0.579897
25% 0.027849 0.162392 2.452280
50% 0.058170 0.275974 2.869549
75% 0.103242 0.418960 3.198405
max 0.622747 1.639550 4.358173

Interpretation: The model regresses ROA on two variables — leverage and ESG score — using 427 S&P 500 firms. Leverage (β = 0.0415, p = 0.022): Statistically significant at the 5% level. Holding ESG constant, a 1 percentage point increase in leverage is associated with a 0.0415 increase in ROA. In economic terms, a one standard deviation increase in leverage (0.211) is associated with a 0.0415 × 0.211 = 0.0088 increase in ROA (about 0.88 pp) — that's 0.0088 / 0.079 ≈ 0.11 standard deviations of ROA. A modest but real effect.

ESG (β = −0.0089, p = 0.187): Not statistically significant at the 5% level — we cannot reject H₀ that ESG has no effect on ROA. A one standard deviation increase in ESG score (0.566) would be associated with a −0.0089 × 0.566 = −0.005 change in ROA (about −0.5 pp), but given the high p-value, this could easily be noise.

R² = 0.016: The two variables together explain only 1.6% of the variation in ROA across firms. Even though leverage is statistically significant, the overall model has very limited explanatory power — the vast majority of ROA differences across S&P 500 firms are driven by other factors not included here (industry, management quality, competitive position, etc.). Bottom line: Leverage has a small but statistically significant positive association with ROA; ESG does not appear to have a meaningful relationship with profitability in this sample. The very low R² tells us that neither variable is a strong driver of cross-sectional ROA differences.

6.4. Correlation ≠ Causation¶

A regression can tell you that leverage and ROA move together — but it cannot, by itself, tell you that leverage causes ROA to change. The relationship could run the other way (profitable firms choose different leverage), or a third variable could drive both (e.g. firm size, industry, or management quality). This is the endogeneity problem, and it is the central challenge of empirical finance.

Classic example: ice cream sales and drowning deaths are highly correlated. The reason is not that ice cream kills people — it is that hot weather drives both. Without accounting for temperature (the confounder), a naive regression would suggest a causal link where none exists.

In corporate finance, the same logic applies everywhere. Do ESG scores improve profitability, or do already-profitable firms simply invest more in ESG? Does leverage boost returns, or do high-return firms take on more debt because they can? OLS alone cannot answer these questions.

Econometricians have developed several strategies to isolate causal effects. The most common in finance and accounting research are:

Instrumental Variables (IV): Find a variable (the "instrument") that affects the independent variable but has no direct effect on the outcome. For example, using a regulatory change that forces firms to adjust leverage, but that does not directly affect ROA through any other channel. The instrument creates quasi-random variation in leverage, allowing you to estimate a causal effect.

Difference-in-Differences (DiD): Compare the change in outcome for a treated group (e.g. firms affected by a new regulation) versus a control group (unaffected firms), before and after the event. The key assumption is that both groups would have followed the same trend absent the treatment (parallel trends).

Regression Discontinuity (RD): Exploit a sharp cutoff rule — for example, firms just above vs just below a threshold for index inclusion — to compare outcomes for nearly identical firms that received different treatments by chance.

The Python packages you can explore are:

Method Package Documentation
IV / 2SLS linearmodels bashtage.github.io/linearmodels
DiD differences github.com/bernardodionisi/differences
DiD (Callaway & Sant'Anna) csdid github.com/d2cml-ai/csdid
RD designs rdrobust rdpackages.github.io
General causal inference DoWhy github.com/py-why/dowhy
Matching / propensity score CausalInference github.com/laurencium/CausalInference

Find below key references for master students. These papers are excellent entry points to understand when and how causality can be established in finance:

  • Roberts, M.R. & Whited, T.M. (2013), "Endogeneity in Empirical Corporate Finance", Handbook of the Economics of Finance. The go-to survey on why OLS is not enough in corporate finance and what tools exist.

  • Angrist, J.D. & Pischke, J.-S. (2009), Mostly Harmless Econometrics, Princeton University Press. The standard reference on IV, DiD, and RD for applied researchers. Accessible and practical.

  • Imbens, G.W. & Wooldridge, J.M. (2009), "Recent Developments in the Econometrics of Program Evaluation", Journal of Economic Literature. Comprehensive overview of modern causal inference methods.

  • Atanasov, V.A. & Black, B.S. (2016), "Shock-Based Causal Inference in Corporate Finance and Accounting Research", Critical Finance Review. Practical guidance on finding and validating natural experiments in finance settings.

  • Roth, J., Sant'Anna, P.H.C., Bilinski, A. & Poe, J. (2023), "What's Trending in Difference-in-Differences?", Journal of Econometrics. State-of-the-art on DiD with staggered treatment timing and diagnostics.

For a master thesis or research project aiming to establish causality:

  1. Start with the identification strategy, not the data. Ideally, ask yourself: what source of quasi-random variation can I exploit? A regulatory shock, a natural disaster, an index reconstitution, a scoring threshold?

  2. Be honest about the assumptions. Every causal method relies on assumptions (exclusion restriction for IV, parallel trends for DiD). State them explicitly and test them where possible.

  3. OLS is still valuable. Even without a causal design, a well-specified OLS with appropriate controls documents meaningful associations. Not every paper needs an instrument — but you must be clear about what you can and cannot claim.

6.5. Year and industry fixed effects¶

pd.get_dummies() is the pandas function that turns a categorical column into a set of binary (0/1) dummy variables — one column per category. For example, a column with values ["Tech", "Finance", "Energy"] becomes three binary columns: ind_Finance, ind_Energy, and (optionally) ind_Tech. The argument drop_first=True removes one category to avoid perfect multicollinearity (the so-called dummy variable trap). We use it below to create industry and year fixed effects.

Fixed effects are dummy variables — one for each category in a grouping variable (e.g. industry or year) — included in a regression to absorb all variation that is specific to that group.

An industry fixed effect means: instead of comparing Tech firms against Finance firms, we only compare firms within the same industry. This removes any time-invariant industry characteristic (regulation, capital intensity, business model) that might drive both the dependent and independent variable simultaneously. If Finance firms naturally have both high leverage and high ROA simply because they are banks, that confound is absorbed by the fixed effect.

A year fixed effect does the same for time: it removes shocks that hit all firms in a given year (a recession, a rate hike, a pandemic). If 2022 was a bad year that pushed both ROA down and leverage up for everyone, that common movement is absorbed — so it does not contaminate the estimated relationship between leverage and ROA.

They are useful because many confounders are unobservable — you cannot directly measure "industry culture" or "macro sentiment." Fixed effects control for all characteristics of a group at once, whether observed or not, without needing to specify them individually. The price is that you can no longer estimate the effect of variables that do not vary within the group (e.g. industry fixed effects absorb anything that is constant across all firms in an industry), but what remains is a cleaner, within-group comparison — often much closer to a causal estimate.

Below, we add industry and year fixed effects to our multivariate regressions using data from the 10 years prior to the event. We first estimate the baseline model, then sequentially add industry fixed effects, year fixed effects, and finally both together, and we comment on the changes in coefficients and the adjusted R². We focus on adjusted R² because adding fixed effects introduces many additional parameters (dummy variables), which mechanically inflates regular R² even if model fit does not meaningfully improve. Adjusted R² corrects for this by penalizing model complexity, allowing us to assess whether the inclusion of industry and time controls provides genuine explanatory power rather than a spurious increase in fit driven by over-parameterization.

In [ ]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import pandas as pd  # pandas is the standard Python library for working with tabular data (DataFrames)
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
from pathlib import Path  # Pathlib helps you build file paths in a cross-platform way
from datetime import datetime, date  # datetime provides date/time objects and parsing
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

rd.open_session()  # rd.open_session() starts an authenticated connection to Refinitiv/LSEG (needed before queries)

# ── 1. S&P 500 constituents with TRBC industry ─────────────────────────────────
sp500_constituents = rd.get_data(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'sp500_constituents' so we can reuse it later
    universe="0#.SPX",
    fields=["TR.RIC", "TR.CompanyName", "TR.TRBCBusinessSector"],  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
    use_field_names_in_headers=True,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'use_field_names_in_headers' for the function call
)  
sp500_constituents.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\sp500_constituents_trbc.csv", index=False)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk
rics_sp500 = sp500_constituents["TR.RIC"].dropna().astype(str).tolist()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'rics_sp500' so we can reuse it later

# ── 2. Pull financials + ESG ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
panel_raw = rd.get_data(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'panel_raw' so we can reuse it later
    universe=rics_sp500,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=[  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
        "TR.NetIncome",  
        "TR.TotalAssets",  
        "TR.TotalDebt", 
        "TR.TRESGScore",  
        "TR.TRESGScore.date",  
    ],  
    parameters={"SDate": "2015-01-01", "EDate": "2024-12-31", "Frq": "FY"},  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'parameters' for the function call
    use_field_names_in_headers=True,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'use_field_names_in_headers' for the function call
) 

rd.close_session()  

#rename
panel = panel_raw.rename(columns = {  # variable assignment: store a value in 'panel' so we can reuse it later
    "TR.NETINCOME":        "net_income", 
    "TR.TOTALASSETS":      "total_assets", 
    "TR.TOTALDEBT":        "total_debt", 
    "TR.TRESGSCORE":       "esg", 
    "TR.TRESGSCORE.DATE":  "date", 
}) 


# ── 4. Build variables + merge industry + extract year ──────────────────────────
for col in ["net_income", "total_assets", "total_debt", "esg"]:  # for-loop: repeat code for each element in an iterable
    panel[col] = pd.to_numeric(panel[col], errors="coerce") 

panel = panel.loc[panel["total_assets"] > 0].copy()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'panel' so we can reuse it later
panel["roa"]      = panel["net_income"] / panel["total_assets"] 
panel["leverage"] = panel["total_debt"] / panel["total_assets"] 

# Year from ESG date
panel["year"] = pd.to_datetime(panel["date"], errors="coerce").dt.year  # pd.to_datetime() converts text/dates into pandas datetime objects

# Merge TRBC sector as industry
panel = panel.merge(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'panel' so we can reuse it later
    sp500_constituents[["TR.RIC", "TR.TRBCBUSINESSSECTOR"]], 
    left_on="Instrument",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'left_on' for the function call
    right_on="TR.RIC",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'right_on' for the function call
    how="left",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'how' for the function call
).rename(columns={"TR.TRBCBUSINESSSECTOR": "industry"}) 

# Drop the duplicate RIC column from the merge
panel = panel.drop(columns=["TR.RIC"], errors="ignore")  # variable assignment: store a value in 'panel' so we can reuse it later

# ── 5. Keep clean regression dataset and save ──────────────────────────────────
panel_out = panel[["Instrument", "year", "industry", "roa", "leverage", "esg"]].dropna().copy()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'panel_out' so we can reuse it later
print(f"Usable firm-year observations: {len(panel_out)}")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

panel_out.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\esg_roa_panel_sp500_2015_2024.csv", index=False)  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk

Usable firm-year observations: 4659
In [ ]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import pandas as pd  # pandas is the standard Python library for working with tabular data (DataFrames)
import statsmodels.api as sm  # statsmodels provides statistical models (e.g., OLS regression)
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

# Load the saved 10-year panel
df = pd.read_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\esg_roa_panel_sp500_2015_2024.csv")  # variable assignment: store a value in 'df' so we can reuse it later

# Make sure core regressors are numeric
for col in ["roa", "leverage", "esg"]:  # for-loop: repeat code for each element in an iterable
    df[col] = pd.to_numeric(df[col], errors="coerce") 

# multiply ROA by 1µ0µ0 for readbility of table
df["roa"] = df["roa"]*100 

# Drop rows with missing values in any regression variable
df = df[["roa", "leverage", "esg", "industry", "year"]].dropna().copy()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'df' so we can reuse it later

y = df["roa"].astype(float)  # variable assignment: store a value in 'y' so we can reuse it later
X_base = df[["leverage", "esg"]].astype(float)  # variable assignment: store a value in 'X_base' so we can reuse it later

# without fixed effects
X = sm.add_constant(pd.concat([X_base], axis=1)).astype(float)  # variable assignment: store a value in 'X' so we can reuse it later
m = sm.OLS(y, X).fit()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'm' so we can reuse it later
print(m.summary())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# Industry fixed effects
ind_dums = pd.get_dummies(df["industry"], prefix="ind", drop_first=True).astype(float)  # variable assignment: store a value in 'ind_dums' so we can reuse it later
X_ind = sm.add_constant(pd.concat([X_base, ind_dums], axis=1)).astype(float)  # variable assignment: store a value in 'X_ind' so we can reuse it later
m_ind = sm.OLS(y, X_ind).fit()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'm_ind' so we can reuse it later
print(m_ind.summary())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# Year fixed effects
year_dums = pd.get_dummies(df["year"], prefix="yr", drop_first=True).astype(float)  # variable assignment: store a value in 'year_dums' so we can reuse it later
X_year = sm.add_constant(pd.concat([X_base, year_dums], axis=1)).astype(float)  # variable assignment: store a value in 'X_year' so we can reuse it later
m_year = sm.OLS(y, X_year).fit()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'm_year' so we can reuse it later
print(m_year.summary())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# Industry + year fixed effects
X_both = sm.add_constant(pd.concat([X_base, ind_dums, year_dums], axis=1)).astype(float)  # variable assignment: store a value in 'X_both' so we can reuse it later
m_both = sm.OLS(y, X_both).fit()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'm_both' so we can reuse it later
print(m_both.summary())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# Comparison table
results = pd.DataFrame({  # variable assignment: store a value in 'results' so we can reuse it later
    "Specification": ["Baseline", "Industry FE", "Year FE", "Industry + Year FE"], 
    "beta_esg":      [m.params["esg"]  for m in (m, m_ind, m_year, m_both)],  # a for-loop repeats a block of code for each element in a sequence
    "p-value":         [m.pvalues["esg"] for m in (m, m_ind, m_year, m_both)],  # a for-loop repeats a block of code for each element in a sequence
    "Adj. R^2":      [m.rsquared_adj   for m in (m, m_ind, m_year, m_both)],  # a for-loop repeats a block of code for each element in a sequence
    "N":             [int(m.nobs)      for m in (m, m_ind, m_year, m_both)],  # a for-loop repeats a block of code for each element in a sequence
}) 
print(results.round(3))  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

                            OLS Regression Results

==============================================================================
Omnibus:                     1668.104   Durbin-Watson:                   1.061
Prob(Omnibus):                  0.000   Jarque-Bera (JB):           671464.122
Skew:                           0.216   Prob(JB):                         0.00
Kurtosis:                      61.811   Cond. No.                     1.56e+04
==============================================================================

Notes:
[1] Standard Errors assume that the covariance matrix of the errors is correctly specified.
[2] The condition number is large, 1.56e+04. This might indicate that there are
strong multicollinearity or other numerical problems.
        Specification  beta_esg  p-value  Adj. R^2     N
0            Baseline     0.013    0.100     0.000  4659
1         Industry FE     0.019    0.012     0.084  4659
2             Year FE     0.007    0.405     0.003  4659
3  Industry + Year FE     0.014    0.090     0.086  4659

Interpretation:

Industry FE are doing the heavy lifting. Without them (baseline), ESG sits at 0.013 (p = 0.10) — borderline nothing. Once you add industry dummies, it jumps to 0.019 and becomes significant at 5% (p = 0.012). This means industry was acting as a negative confounder: sectors with structurally higher ROA (e.g. tech, consumer products — you can see those large positive industry coefficients) tend to have different ESG profiles than lower-ROA sectors (utilities, banking, energy). Pooling everything together mixes up the industry-level ROA differences with the within-industry ESG–ROA relationship, suppressing the coefficient.

Year FE do the opposite. They cut the ESG coefficient roughly in half (0.007, p = 0.41). This suggests a common time trend — both average ESG scores and average ROA have drifted over 2013–2024, creating a spurious positive correlation. Year dummies soak that up, and the within-year ESG signal is essentially zero.

Combined, the two effects partially cancel out (0.014, p = 0.09). Industry FE push the coefficient up, year FE push it down, and you land back near borderline significance.

By the same token, one may want to add for firm fixed effects (evething that is specific to a firm and time invariant would be controlled for). Then, the variation of ESG score within the same firm would be related to the variation of ROA within that same firm.

6.6. Explaining performance during the COVID crisis¶

Application. Now it is your turn to practice. You are asked to examine whether standard characteristics of firms are associated with performance during the COVID crisis. The universe of interest is the CAC All Tradable. Consider as many variables as you deem relevant ex ante to explain performance during this crisis (financial constraints, ESG score, industry). Restart from the dataset built in 5.5: cacall_datasets_full.csv.

To help you, building on the following paper, we set the COVID crisis period in France as February 20 to March 1 2020. You can also use that paper to guide your choice of the frim characteristics to consider. A (hidden) solution is provided below.

In [72]:
import pandas as pd 
import numpy as np
import statsmodels.api as sm  # statsmodels provides statistical models (e.g., OLS regression)

# load data
dataset = pd.read_csv("cacall_datasets_full.csv")

#extra variable
dataset["ln_market_cap"]=np.log(dataset["market_cap"])

# keep non-missing observations
dataset=dataset.dropna()

# generate fixed effects
ind_dums = pd.get_dummies(dataset["industry"], prefix="ind", drop_first=True).astype(float)  # variable assignment: store a value in 'ind_dums' so we can reuse it later

# X and Y for reg
X  = dataset[["ln_market_cap", "esg_score","profitability", "cash_ratio", "leverage"]]
Y = dataset['TR.TOTALRETURN']

# Reg
X_ext = sm.add_constant(pd.concat([X, ind_dums], axis=1)).astype(float)  # variable assignment: store a value in 'X_both' so we can reuse it later
reg = sm.OLS(Y, X_ext).fit()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'm_both' so we can reuse it later
print(reg.summary())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

7. Event Study¶

7.1. What is an event study?¶

In empirical finance, an event study measures how a specific event (e.g. earnings announcement, merger news, index inclusion, ESG controversy) affects stock prices. The core idea is to compare a security’s actual return around the event date with the “normal” return we would have expected if the event had not happened, and to test whether the difference is statistically significant.

Formally, for firm $i$ on day $t$:

  • Actual return: $R_{it}$
  • Expected (normal) return: $\hat{R}_{it}$ from a model estimated before the event
  • Abnormal return: $AR_{it} = R_{it} - \hat{R}_{it}$

We usually sum abnormal returns over an event window (e.g. $[-1,+1]$ days around the event) to get cumulative abnormal returns (CARs) and test whether CARs differ from zero.

7.2. Typical steps in an event study¶

  1. Define the event and sample

    • What is the event? (e.g. ESG rating downgrade, M&A announcement, dividend cut)
    • Which firms? (e.g. all S&P 500 firms with such an event)
    • Event date $t=0$: the announcement or information release date.

  2. Choose estimation and event windows

    • Estimation window: e.g. $[-250,-30]$ trading days before $t=0$, used to estimate the normal return model.
    • Event window: e.g. $[-1,+1]$, $[0,+2]$, or $[-5,+5]$, where you measure the impact.

  3. Estimate normal (expected) returns

    • The most common model is the market model: $$ R_{it} = \alpha_i + \beta_i R_{mt} + \varepsilon_{it} $$ where $R_{mt}$ is the market (index) return.
    • Estimate $\alpha_i$ and $\beta_i$ by OLS over the estimation window.

  4. Compute abnormal and cumulative abnormal returns

    • What "abnormal" means: The abnormal return is the part of the stock's return that is on top of (in excess of) what we would expect from the market alone. The market model (step 3) gives us that expectation: in normal times the stock's return is roughly $\alpha_i + \beta_i R_{mt}$. So the expected return on day $t$ is $\hat{\alpha}_i + \hat{\beta}_i R_{mt}$. Anything above or below that is "abnormal" — i.e. not explained by the usual market relationship, and thus potentially due to the event.
    • Why we compute it this way: By subtracting the market-model prediction, we remove the part of the return that would have happened anyway because the market moved. What remains is the part we attribute to the event (e.g. announcement, shock). We then sum these daily abnormal returns over the event window to get the cumulative abnormal return (CAR) — the total "extra" return over the window.
    • Abnormal return: $AR_{it} = R_{it} - \hat{\alpha}_i - \hat{\beta}_i R_{mt}$ (actual return minus expected return).
    • Cumulative abnormal return (CAR): $CAR_i(\tau_1,\tau_2) = \sum_{t=\tau_1}^{\tau_2} AR_{it}$ — sum of abnormal returns over the event window.
    • Repeat for all firms in the event sample.

  5. Test statistical significance

    • Time-series for a single firm: is $CAR_i$ significantly different from zero?
    • Cross-section of events: is the average CAR across all events significantly different from zero (e.g. t-test on mean CAR)?

7.3. Trump's "Liberation Day" tariff announcement¶

Below we show an example with Refinitiv. We study the event: Trump's "Liberation Day" tariff announcement (April 2, 2025) — the biggest market shock since COVID. On April 2, 2025, President Trump announced sweeping "reciprocal" tariffs from the White House Rose Garden — a 10% baseline on nearly all imports, with rates up to 54% on specific countries (China: 34% + existing 20%, Vietnam: 46%, EU: 20%, Taiwan: 32%). The announcement was made after market close. April 3 was the first full trading day of market reaction. Over April 3–4 alone, the S&P 500 fell approximately 10.5% and the Nasdaq dropped 11.8%, erasing roughly $6.6 trillion in market value. This was the fifth-largest two-day decline in the S&P 500 since 1950.

We expect firms that rely on imported components, raw materials, or finished goods for resale face higher costs. If they cannot fully pass these through to customers, their profit margins shrink. Hence, firms with heavy import dependence (especially from high-tariff countries like China and Vietnam) should exhibit the most negative abnormal returns. Sectors like technology hardware, apparel/footwear, and consumer electronics — where global supply chains are deep — should be hit hardest.

Within the S&P 500, we pick these 10 stocks to analyze:

# Company Ticker RIC Expected CAR Sector Rationale
1 Apple AAPL AAPL.O Negative Technology Hardware Final assembly overwhelmingly in China (Foxconn, Pegatron). 54% China tariff on finished electronics. Cannot relocate iPhone production quickly.
2 Nike NKE NKE.N Negative Apparel & Footwear 50% of footwear from Vietnam (46% tariff), 27% China (54%), 15% apparel Cambodia (49%). Triply exposed to the three highest-tariff countries. Anticipated $1B cost hit.
3 Five Below FIVE FIVE.OQ Negative Discount Retail ~60–70% of inventory sourced from China. Razor-thin margins, price-sensitive customers → near-zero ability to absorb or pass through cost increases.
4 Ford F F.N Negative Automobiles Heavily integrated Mexico/Canada supply chain (25% tariff). Parts cross the border multiple times → "cost-stacking". Also exposed to China retaliation on exports.
5 JPMorgan Chase JPM JPM.N Neutral Banking No physical imports or supply-chain exposure. Indirect effects only (credit quality, deal flow). Should track market beta with minimal firm-specific AR.
6 UnitedHealth UNH UNH.N Neutral Healthcare / Insurance Revenue from domestic premiums and government programmes. No imported goods. Pharma tariffs not yet announced on Liberation Day.
7 Waste Management WM WM.N Neutral Domestic Services Purely domestic business (trash collection, landfills). Zero import/export exposure. Defensive stock with no tariff channel.
8 Nucor NUE NUE.N Positive Steel (Domestic) Largest US steel producer, 100% domestic production. Steel tariffs (25–50%) cut imports by 35% → gains market share and pricing power. Tariffs are a direct competitive advantage.
9 First Solar FSLR FSLR.OQ Positive Solar (Domestic) Only major solar manufacturer with US-based plants (OH, AL, LA). 54% tariff prices out Chinese panel competitors. Gained ~5% in April while market crashed.
10 Lockheed Martin LMT LMT.N Positive Defence ~100% revenue from US/allied government contracts. Domestic supply chain. Zero import dependence, zero retaliation risk. Relative safe haven in broad selloffs.
In [73]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import pandas as pd  # pandas is the standard Python library for working with tabular data (DataFrames)
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt  # matplotlib is a plotting library for charts and figures
import statsmodels.api as sm  # statsmodels provides statistical models (e.g., OLS regression)
from scipy import stats  # SciPy stats includes statistical tests and distributions
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
from pathlib import Path  # Pathlib helps you build file paths in a cross-platform way
from datetime import datetime, date  # datetime provides date/time objects and parsing
import os  # os provides operating-system utilities (paths, directories, environment variables)

# --- Define any configuration variables used in this cell ---
BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()  # use the current working directory as a default base folder

rd.open_session()  # open a session so we can request data from the platform

# ---------- Event definition ----------
# Trump announced tariffs after market close on April 2, 2025.
# The first FULL trading day of reaction is April 3 → that is our t = 0.
EVENT_DATE = pd.Timestamp("2025-04-03")  # variable assignment: store a value in 'EVENT_DATE' so we can reuse it later

# ---------- Windows (in trading days relative to t = 0) ----------
# Estimation window: used to estimate "normal" returns (the market model).
#   Convention: end it a few days before the event window to avoid
#   contamination from anticipation / leaks.
EST_START = -250      # ≈ 1 year of trading days before the event
EST_END   =  -11      # stop 10 days before the event

# Event window: the period over which we measure abnormal returns.
EVT_START =   -1      # 1 days before the announcement
EVT_END   =   1     # 1 days after (captures the April 9 tariff pause)

# ---------- Securities to study ----------
# We pick stocks with very different tariff exposures:
STOCK_RICS = [  # variable assignment: store a value in 'STOCK_RICS' so we can reuse it later
    "AAPL.O", "NKE.N", "FIVE.OQ", "F.N",       # expected negative
    "JPM.N",  "UNH.N", "WM.N",                  # expected neutral
    "NUE.N",  "FSLR.OQ", "LMT.N",              # expected positive
] 
NAMES = {  # variable assignment: store a value in 'NAMES' so we can reuse it later
    "AAPL.O": "Apple",       "NKE.N":   "Nike", 
    "FIVE.OQ":"Five Below",  "F.N":     "Ford", 
    "JPM.N":  "JPMorgan",    "UNH.N":   "UnitedHealth", 
    "WM.N":   "Waste Mgmt",  "NUE.N":   "Nucor", 
    "FSLR.OQ":"First Solar", "LMT.N":   "Lockheed Martin", 
} 

# Market benchmark (S&P 500 total-return index)
#    .SPXTR  = S&P 500 Total Return (gross, reinvests dividends)
MARKET_RIC = ".SPXTR"  # variable assignment: store a value in 'MARKET_RIC' so we can reuse it later

# ---------- Data range ----------
# We need enough history for the estimation window, plus the event window.
# Fetch a generous range to be safe.
DATA_START = "2024-01-01"  # variable assignment: store a value in 'DATA_START' so we can reuse it later
DATA_END   = "2025-04-30"  # variable assignment: store a value in 'DATA_END' so we can reuse it later

STOCK_NAMES = {  # variable assignment: store a value in 'STOCK_NAMES' so we can reuse it later
    "AAPL.O": "Apple", 
    "NKE.N":  "Nike", 
    "LMT.N":  "Lockheed Martin", 
} 

#  1.  DOWNLOAD PRICE DATA FROM REFINITIV 
print("=" * 70)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print("STEP 1 — Downloading daily price data from Refinitiv")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print("=" * 70)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
rd.open_session()  # rd.open_session() starts an authenticated connection to Refinitiv/LSEG (needed before queries)


# ── Stocks: daily total returns (includes dividends) ──────────────
stock_returns = rd.get_data(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'stock_returns' so we can reuse it later
    universe=STOCK_RICS,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=["TR.TOTALRETURN", "TR.TOTALRETURN.date"],  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
    parameters={"SDate": DATA_START, "EDate": DATA_END, "Frq": "D"},  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'parameters' for the function call
    use_field_names_in_headers=True,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'use_field_names_in_headers' for the function call
) 

# ── Market benchmark: use the S&P 500 TOTAL RETURN index ─────────
MARKET_RIC = ".SPXTR"  # variable assignment: store a value in 'MARKET_RIC' so we can reuse it later
print(stock_returns)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

market_prices = rd.get_history(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'market_prices' so we can reuse it later
    universe=MARKET_RIC,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=["TRDPRC_1"],  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
    interval="daily",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'interval' for the function call
    start=DATA_START,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'start' for the function call
    end=DATA_END,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'end' for the function call
) 

# return calculation
market_returns = (market_prices / market_prices.shift(1) - 1).dropna()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'market_returns' so we can reuse it later
print(market_returns)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

rd.close_session() 

# ── Clean up ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

# --- Stocks: long → wide (Date × RIC), convert % to decimal ---------------
stock_returns["Date"] = pd.to_datetime(stock_returns["TR.TOTALRETURN.DATE"])  # pd.to_datetime() converts text/dates into pandas datetime objects
stock_returns["ret"]  = pd.to_numeric(stock_returns["TR.TOTALRETURN"], errors="coerce") / 100 

stock_ret = (  # variable assignment: store a value in 'stock_ret' so we can reuse it later
    stock_returns.pivot(index="Date", columns="Instrument", values="ret") 
    .sort_index() 
    .astype(float)       # ← add this
) 

# --- Market: simple return from index level --------------------------------
market_prices = market_prices.apply(pd.to_numeric, errors="coerce").dropna()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'market_prices' so we can reuse it later
market_prices.index = pd.to_datetime(market_prices.index)  # pd.to_datetime() converts text/dates into pandas datetime objects
mkt_ret = (market_prices / market_prices.shift(1) - 1).dropna()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'mkt_ret' so we can reuse it later
mkt_ret.columns = ["Rm"] 
mkt_ret = mkt_ret.astype(float)       # ← add this

# --- Merge on common dates ------------------------------------------------
returns = stock_ret.join(mkt_ret, how="inner").dropna()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'returns' so we can reuse it later
print(f"\nReturn matrix: {returns.shape[0]} days × {returns.shape[1]} cols")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print(returns.head())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

#save 
returns.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\returns_for_event_study.csv")  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on disk

======================================================================
STEP 1 — Downloading daily price data from Refinitiv

2024-01-03 -0.007488 -0.037007 -0.050206 -0.027533 -0.004358  0.006577   
2024-01-04 -0.012700 -0.002562  0.001319 -0.006630  0.006595 -0.002723   
2024-01-05 -0.004013  0.014555 -0.020101  0.003367  0.005017 -0.002992   
2024-01-08  0.024175  0.010127 -0.039634 -0.004435 -0.001451  0.004600   
2024-01-09 -0.002263 -0.010860 -0.009695  0.004695 -0.007906 -0.005037   

               NKE.N     NUE.N     UNH.N      WM.N        Rm  
Date                                                          
2024-01-03 -0.023557  0.006498  0.004988 -0.006013 -0.007974  
2024-01-04 -0.016724 -0.025486  0.006254  0.001456 -0.003298  
2024-01-05 -0.002151 -0.008717 -0.014741 -0.005705  0.001828  
2024-01-08  0.015086  0.012781 -0.001600  0.002644  0.014132

In [ ]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import pandas as pd  # pandas is the standard Python library for working with tabular data (DataFrames)
import numpy as np  # NumPy provides fast numerical arrays and math functions
import statsmodels.api as sm  # statsmodels provides statistical models (e.g., OLS regression)
from scipy import stats  # SciPy stats includes statistical tests and distributions

# =============================================================================
#  3. EVENT TIME τ
# =============================================================================

dates  = returns.index.sort_values()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'dates' so we can reuse it later
t0_idx = dates.searchsorted(EVENT_DATE)  # variable assignment: store a value in 't0_idx' so we can reuse it later
returns["tau"] = np.arange(len(dates)) - t0_idx 

est = returns[returns["tau"].between(EST_START, EST_END)]  # variable assignment: store a value in 'est' so we can reuse it later
evt = returns[returns["tau"].between(EVT_START, EVT_END)].copy()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'evt' so we can reuse it later
print(f"Estimation: {len(est)} days  |  Event: {len(evt)} days")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# =============================================================================
#  4. MARKET MODEL → AR → CAR
# =============================================================================

models = {}  # variable assignment: store a value in 'models' so we can reuse it later
for ric in STOCK_RICS:  # for-loop: repeat code for each element in an iterable
    X = sm.add_constant(est["Rm"])  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'X' for the function call
    ols = sm.OLS(est[ric], X, missing="drop").fit()  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'ols' for the function call
    models[ric] = ols 
    evt[f"AR_{ric}"]  = evt[ric] - (ols.params["const"] + ols.params["Rm"] * evt["Rm"]) 
    evt[f"CAR_{ric}"] = evt[f"AR_{ric}"].cumsum() 

# =============================================================================
#  5. RESULTS TABLE  (t-test, α = 5%)
# =============================================================================

rows = []  # variable assignment: store a value in 'rows' so we can reuse it later
for ric in STOCK_RICS:  # for-loop: repeat code for each element in an iterable
    sigma  = np.sqrt(models[ric].mse_resid)  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'sigma' for the function call
    df     = int(models[ric].nobs) - 2  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'df' for the function call
    car    = evt[f"AR_{ric}"].sum()  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'car' for the function call
    t_stat = car / (sigma * np.sqrt(len(evt)))  # keyword argument: set the parameter 't_stat' for the function call
    p_val  = 2 * stats.t.sf(abs(t_stat), df=df)  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'p_val' for the function call

    if p_val < 0.05 and car > 0:   reaction = "POSITIVE"  # if-statement: run the indented block only if the condition is True
    elif p_val < 0.05 and car < 0: reaction = "NEGATIVE" 
    else:                           reaction = "NEUTRAL" 

    rows.append(dict( 
        Stock=NAMES[ric], alpha=f"{models[ric].params['const']:.6f}",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'Stock' for the function call
        beta=f"{models[ric].params['Rm']:.4f}", R2=f"{models[ric].rsquared:.4f}",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'beta' for the function call
        CAR_pct=f"{car*100:+.2f}%", t_stat=f"{t_stat:+.3f}",  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'CAR_pct' for the function call
        p_value=f"{p_val:.4f}", Reaction=reaction))  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'p_value' for the function call

print(pd.DataFrame(rows).to_string(index=False))  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print(f"\nWindow: CAR[{EVT_START},{EVT_END}] | α = 5%")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

Estimation: 240 days  |  Event: 3 days
          Stock     alpha    beta     R2 CAR_pct t_stat p_value Reaction
          Apple  0.000704  0.9573 0.2908  -6.74% -2.956  0.0034 NEGATIVE
           Nike -0.000806  0.5162 0.0459  -5.66% -1.575  0.1165  NEUTRAL
     Five Below -0.003001  0.7457 0.0389 -16.31% -2.886  0.0043 NEGATIVE
           Ford -0.001093  0.9838 0.1439  +6.81% +1.860  0.0642  NEUTRAL
       JPMorgan  0.000596  0.9114 0.2433  -4.47% -1.825  0.0693  NEUTRAL
   UnitedHealth  0.000506  0.2354 0.0127  +2.69% +0.851  0.3956  NEUTRAL
     Waste Mgmt  0.000286  0.3771 0.0755  +0.12% +0.058  0.9538  NEUTRAL
          Nucor -0.001925  1.0069 0.1602  -3.16% -0.899  0.3698  NEUTRAL
    First Solar -0.000895  0.9783 0.0604 +11.34% +1.926  0.0553  NEUTRAL
Lockheed Martin  0.000434 -0.0737 0.0024  -4.49% -1.970  0.0500  NEUTRAL

Window: CAR[-1,1] | α = 5%

To assess statistical significance of the results, here we use two-sided tests as a conservative choice. Our hypotheses are directional — negative CARs expected for import-exposed firms, positive for tariff-protected ones — so one-sided tests would formally be appropriate. However, two-sided tests are more stringent and less open to the criticism of cherry-picking which tail to test.

# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import pandas as pd  # pandas is the standard Python library for working with tabular data (DataFrames)
import numpy as np  # NumPy provides fast numerical arrays and math functions
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt  # matplotlib is a plotting library for charts and figures
# =============================================================================
#  CHART B — AR heatmap
# =============================================================================

order = [ric for rics in groups.values() for ric in rics]  # variable assignment: store a value in 'order' so we can reuse it later
ar_matrix = pd.DataFrame(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'ar_matrix' so we can reuse it later
    {NAMES[ric]: evt.set_index("tau")[f"AR_{ric}"] * 100 for ric in order}  # a for-loop repeats a block of code for each element in a sequence
).T 

fig2, ax2 = plt.subplots(figsize=(16, 5)) 
vmax = np.ceil(ar_matrix.abs().max().max())  # variable assignment: store a value in 'vmax' so we can reuse it later
im = ax2.imshow(ar_matrix.values, aspect="auto", cmap="RdYlGn", vmin=-vmax, vmax=vmax)  # variable assignment: store a value in 'im' so we can reuse it later

ax2.set_xticks(range(len(ar_matrix.columns))) 
ax2.set_xticklabels([f"τ={int(t)}" for t in ar_matrix.columns], fontsize=8)  # a for-loop repeats a block of code for each element in a sequence
ax2.set_yticks(range(len(ar_matrix.index))) 
ax2.set_yticklabels(ar_matrix.index, fontsize=10) 

for i in range(ar_matrix.shape[0]):  # for-loop: repeat code for each element in an iterable
    for j in range(ar_matrix.shape[1]):  # for-loop: repeat code for each element in an iterable
        val = ar_matrix.iloc[i, j]  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'val' for the function call
        ax2.text(j, i, f"{val:+.1f}", ha="center", va="center", fontsize=7, 
                 color="white" if abs(val) > vmax * 0.6 else "black", fontweight="bold")  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'color' for the function call

ax2.axhline(3.5, color="black", lw=1.5) 
ax2.axhline(6.5, color="black", lw=1.5) 
ax2.axvline(list(ar_matrix.columns).index(0) - 0.5, color="red", lw=2, ls="--", alpha=.7) 
plt.colorbar(im, ax=ax2, shrink=0.8, pad=0.02, label="Abnormal Return (%)") 
ax2.set_title('Daily Abnormal Returns — Heatmap', fontweight="bold") 
plt.tight_layout() 
plt.savefig("event_study_ar_heatmap.png", dpi=200) 
plt.show() 

# =============================================================================
#  CHART C — CAR bar chart (full window)
# =============================================================================

cars = pd.Series({NAMES[ric]: evt[f"AR_{ric}"].sum() * 100 for ric in order})  # variable assignment: store a value in 'cars' so we can reuse it later

fig3, ax3 = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5)) 
colors = ["#d62728" if v < 0 else "#2ca02c" for v in cars]
bars = ax3.barh(range(len(cars)), cars.values, color=colors, edgecolor="white", height=0.7)  # variable assignment: store a value in 'bars' so we can reuse it later
ax3.set_yticks(range(len(cars))) 
ax3.set_yticklabels(cars.index, fontsize=10) 
ax3.axvline(0, color="black", lw=.8) 
ax3.set_xlabel("CAR (%)") 
ax3.set_title(f'CAR[{EVT_START},{EVT_END}] — "Liberation Day"', fontweight="bold") 
ax3.grid(axis="x", alpha=.3) 
ax3.invert_yaxis() 

for i, (v, bar) in enumerate(zip(cars.values, bars)):  # for-loop: repeat code for each element in an iterable
    ax3.text(v + (0.3 if v >= 0 else -0.3), i, f"{v:+.1f}%",  # an if-statement runs code only when a condition is true
             va="center", ha="left" if v >= 0 else "right", fontsize=9, fontweight="bold")  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'va' for the function call

plt.tight_layout() 
plt.savefig("event_study_car_bars.png", dpi=200) 
plt.show()

7.4. Market reaction to the Paris Agreement (2015)¶

Application. Now it is your turn to practice. You are to examine the market reaction to the adoption of the Paris Agreement on the stock price of US fossil fuel firms and banks known to finance fossil fuels. See below for the hypothesis, guidance, and a (hidden) possible solution.

The Paris Agreement was adopted at COP21 on December 12, 2015 in Paris, setting a legally binding international framework to limit global warming. December 12 was a Saturday, so the first full trading day of market reaction is December 14, 2015 (t = 0).

Hypothesis¶

The Paris Agreement signals a credible international commitment to decarbonisation. For firms whose revenues or business model depend on fossil fuels, this introduces new risks: future carbon taxes, stricter emissions regulations, potential stranded assets (reserves that may never be burned), and growing pressure on lenders to restrict fossil fuel financing. We therefore expect negative abnormal returns for both groups:

  1. US fossil fuel firms — the agreement signals higher future carbon costs, potential production restrictions, and the risk that part of their proven reserves become economically unviable (stranded assets).
  2. US banks heavily exposed to fossil fuel financing — the agreement increases regulatory and reputational pressure on fossil fuel lending, raising the prospect of future restrictions on their loan books and forcing a reassessment of climate-related credit risk.

This hypothesis is supported by Diaz-Rainey, Gehricke, Roberts & Zhang (2021) (International Review of Financial Analysis, Vol. 76), who find a cumulative average abnormal return (CAAR) of −8.4% for US-listed oil and gas firms around the Paris Agreement signing, with the most negative reactions in E&P (−12.2%) and drilling (−10.5%) sub-sectors.

We pick 10 US publicly listed stocks to analyse:

# Company Ticker RIC Expected CAR Group Rationale
1 ExxonMobil XOM XOM.N Negative Oil Major Largest US oil major. Significant proved reserves at risk of becoming stranded under future carbon constraints.
2 Chevron CVX CVX.N Negative Oil Major Second largest US oil major. High capex in long-cycle fossil fuel projects now exposed to carbon risk.
3 ConocoPhillips COP COP.N Negative E&P Pure-play E&P with no downstream buffer. Fully exposed to carbon-driven production constraints.
4 Devon Energy DVN DVN.N Negative E&P US shale-focused E&P. Shale economics depend on regulatory permissiveness, now threatened.
5 Peabody Energy BTU BTU.N Negative Coal Largest US publicly listed coal producer. Coal is the most carbon-intensive fuel — directly in the crosshairs of the agreement.
6 JPMorgan Chase JPM JPM.N Negative Bank World's #1 fossil fuel financier. Paris Agreement increases regulatory and ESG pressure on fossil fuel lending.
7 Citigroup C C.N Negative Bank #2 US fossil fuel lender. Same channel as JPM — raised expected cost of fossil fuel credit risk.
8 Bank of America BAC BAC.N Negative Bank #3 US fossil fuel lender. International climate commitment raises prospect of future restrictions on loan books.
9 Wells Fargo WFC WFC.N Negative Bank Major fossil fuel lender. Growing ESG investor pressure on banks financing high-carbon sectors.
10 Goldman Sachs GS GS.N Negative Bank Major fossil fuel arranger and advisor. Climate commitments reduce deal flow and raise reputational costs.
In [77]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import statsmodels.api as sm
from scipy import stats
import refinitiv.data as rd
import os

BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()

# ---------- Event definition ----------
# The Paris Agreement was adopted at COP21 on December 12, 2015 (Saturday, markets closed).
# First full trading day of reaction: December 14, 2015 — that is t = 0.
EVENT_DATE = pd.Timestamp("2015-12-14")

# ---------- Windows (in trading days relative to t = 0) ----------
EST_START = -250    # ≈ 1 year of trading days before the event
EST_END   =  -11    # stop 10 days before the event window

EVT_START =   -1    # 1 day before (anticipation / leakage)
EVT_END   =   +3    # 3 days after

# ---------- Securities ----------
STOCK_RICS = [
    "XOM.N", "CVX.N", "COP.N", "DVN.N", "BTU.N",   # fossil fuel firms
    "JPM.N", "C.N",   "BAC.N", "WFC.N", "GS.N",    # banks financing fossil fuels
]
NAMES = {
    "XOM.N": "ExxonMobil",    "CVX.N": "Chevron",
    "COP.N": "ConocoPhillips","DVN.N": "Devon Energy",
    "BTU.N": "Peabody Energy","JPM.N": "JPMorgan",
    "C.N":   "Citigroup",     "BAC.N": "Bank of America",
    "WFC.N": "Wells Fargo",   "GS.N":  "Goldman Sachs",
}

MARKET_RIC = ".SPXTR"
DATA_START  = "2014-06-01"
DATA_END    = "2016-03-31"

print("=" * 70)
print("STEP 1 — Downloading daily price data from Refinitiv")
print("=" * 70)
rd.open_session()

# ── Stocks: daily total returns ────────────────────────────────────
stock_returns = rd.get_data(
    universe=STOCK_RICS,
    fields=["TR.TOTALRETURN", "TR.TOTALRETURN.date"],
    parameters={"SDate": DATA_START, "EDate": DATA_END, "Frq": "D"},
    use_field_names_in_headers=True,
)
print(stock_returns)

# ── Market benchmark: S&P 500 total return ─────────────────────────
market_prices = rd.get_history(
    universe=MARKET_RIC,
    fields=["TRDPRC_1"],
    interval="daily",
    start=DATA_START,
    end=DATA_END,
)
print(market_prices)
rd.close_session()

# ── Clean up ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
stock_returns["Date"] = pd.to_datetime(stock_returns["TR.TOTALRETURN.DATE"])
stock_returns["ret"]  = pd.to_numeric(stock_returns["TR.TOTALRETURN"], errors="coerce") / 100

stock_ret = (
    stock_returns.pivot_table(index="Date", columns="Instrument", values="ret", aggfunc="mean")
    .sort_index()
    .astype(float)
)

market_prices = market_prices.apply(pd.to_numeric, errors="coerce").dropna()
market_prices.index = pd.to_datetime(market_prices.index)
mkt_ret = (market_prices / market_prices.shift(1) - 1).dropna()
mkt_ret.columns = ["Rm"]
mkt_ret = mkt_ret.astype(float)

returns = stock_ret.join(mkt_ret, how="inner").dropna()
print(f"\nReturn matrix: {returns.shape[0]} days × {returns.shape[1]} cols")
print(returns.head())

returns.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\returns_paris_agreement_2015.csv")

======================================================================
STEP 1 — Downloading daily price data from Refinitiv
======================================================================

               JPM.N     WFC.N     XOM.N        Rm  
Date

2014-06-04  0.001439 -0.000979 -0.003486  0.002070
