In [81]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import statsmodels.api as sm
from scipy import stats

# ── Event time index ──────────────────────────────────────────────
dates  = returns.index.sort_values()
t0_idx = dates.searchsorted(EVENT_DATE)
returns["tau"] = range(-t0_idx, len(dates) - t0_idx)

est = returns[returns["tau"].between(EST_START, EST_END)]
evt = returns[returns["tau"].between(EVT_START, EVT_END)].copy()

print(f"Estimation: {len(est)} days  |  Event: {len(evt)} days")

# ── Market model OLS + CAR ────────────────────────────────────────
results   = []
ar_matrix = {}

STOCK_RICS = [r for r in STOCK_RICS if r in returns.columns and returns[r].dropna().shape[0] > EST_END - EST_START]

for ric in STOCK_RICS:
    X_est = sm.add_constant(est["Rm"])
    y_est = est[ric]
    model = sm.OLS(y_est, X_est).fit()
    alpha, beta = model.params["const"], model.params["Rm"]
    r2 = model.rsquared

    ar = evt[ric] - (alpha + beta * evt["Rm"])
    ar_matrix[NAMES[ric]] = ar.values
    car = ar.sum()

    ar_est = y_est - (alpha + beta * est["Rm"])
    se_car = ar_est.std() * np.sqrt(len(evt))
    t_stat = car / se_car
    p_val  = 2 * stats.t.sf(abs(t_stat), df=len(est) - 2)
    reaction = "POSITIVE" if car > 0 and p_val < 0.05 else (
               "NEGATIVE" if car < 0 and p_val < 0.05 else "NEUTRAL")

    results.append(dict(
        Stock=NAMES[ric], alpha=round(alpha, 6), beta=round(beta, 4),
        R2=round(r2, 4), CAR_pct=f"{car*100:+.2f}%",
        t_stat=f"{t_stat:+.3f}", p_value=round(p_val, 4), Reaction=reaction
    ))

res_df = pd.DataFrame(results)
print(res_df.to_string(index=False))
print(f"\nWindow: CAR[{EVT_START},{EVT_END}] | α = 5%")

# ── Plots ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
fig, (ax1, ax2, ax3) = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(16, 5))

ar_df  = pd.DataFrame(ar_matrix, index=range(EVT_START, EVT_END + 1))
cum_ar = ar_df.cumsum()
for col in cum_ar.columns:
    ax1.plot(cum_ar.index, cum_ar[col] * 100, marker="o", label=col)
ax1.axhline(0, color="black", linewidth=0.8, linestyle="--")
ax1.axvline(0, color="red",   linewidth=0.8, linestyle="--", label="Event (t=0)")
ax1.set_title("Cumulative Abnormal Returns\nParis Agreement Adoption (2015)", fontweight="bold")
ax1.set_xlabel("Trading days relative to event")
ax1.set_ylabel("CAR (%)")
ax1.legend(fontsize=6, ncol=2)

cars   = [float(r["CAR_pct"].replace("%","")) for r in results]
names  = [r["Stock"] for r in results]
colors = ["green" if c > 0 else "red" for c in cars]
ax2.barh(names, cars, color=colors)
ax2.axvline(0, color="black", linewidth=0.8)
ax2.set_title(f'CAR[{EVT_START},{EVT_END}] — Paris Agreement (2015)', fontweight="bold")
ax2.set_xlabel("CAR (%)")

groups = ["Fossil fuel"] * 5 + ["Bank"] * 5
gcols  = ["saddlebrown" if g == "Fossil fuel" else "steelblue" for g in groups]
ax3.barh(names, cars, color=gcols)
ax3.axvline(0, color="black", linewidth=0.8)
ax3.set_title("CAR by group\n(brown = FF firm, blue = bank)", fontweight="bold")
ax3.set_xlabel("CAR (%)")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Estimation: 240 days  |  Event: 5 days
          Stock     alpha   beta     R2 CAR_pct t_stat  p_value Reaction
     ExxonMobil -0.000405 1.0782 0.5667  +3.75% +1.842   0.0667  NEUTRAL
        Chevron -0.000535 1.2073 0.4688  +2.37% +0.855   0.3935  NEUTRAL
 ConocoPhillips -0.000631 1.2747 0.4008  -2.98% -0.884   0.3774  NEUTRAL
   Devon Energy -0.000819 1.4254 0.3539 -18.08% -4.347   0.0000 NEGATIVE
       JPMorgan  0.000335 1.1938 0.7056  +1.45% +0.869   0.3859  NEUTRAL
      Citigroup -0.000185 1.2628 0.6452  +1.11% +0.547   0.5852  NEUTRAL
Bank of America -0.000089 1.2110 0.5447  +1.28% +0.534   0.5940  NEUTRAL
    Wells Fargo -0.000007 1.0854 0.7214  +2.59% +1.780   0.0764  NEUTRAL
  Goldman Sachs -0.000146 1.1524 0.6854  +0.96% +0.570   0.5690  NEUTRAL

Window: CAR[-1,3] | α = 5%

Interpreting the results in light of the literature¶

Our results over the CAR[−1, +3] window are largely neutral, with one striking exception: Devon Energy shows a large and highly significant negative CAR of −18.08% (t = −4.35, p < 0.001). All other firms — both fossil fuel majors and banks — display small, statistically insignificant abnormal returns. This pattern is informative and consistent with the academic literature on climate policy risk.

Why mostly neutral reactions for large diversified firms? Bolton & Kacperczyk (2021) — "Do Investors Care about Carbon Risk?", Journal of Financial Economics, Vol. 142, pp. 517–549 — show that investors do price carbon transition risk, but the mechanism operates primarily through risk premia in expected returns rather than through immediate price drops at policy announcements. High-emission firms already embed a carbon risk premium in their expected returns; when a climate agreement is announced, markets update this premium gradually rather than producing a sharp one-time price adjustment. Additionally, the COP21 negotiations were highly publicised and ran for years: most of the informational content of the Paris Agreement had been anticipated and progressively priced in well before December 12, 2015, leaving little residual surprise for short-window event studies to capture.

Why is Devon Energy the exception? Devon Energy is a pure-play US shale E&P company with no downstream activities and no international diversification — the most exposed firm in our sample to US domestic regulatory risk. Bolton & Kacperczyk (2023) — "Global Pricing of Carbon-Transition Risk", Journal of Finance, Vol. 78, pp. 3677–3754 — document that carbon transition risk is most severe for firms in sectors with the highest direct emission intensity and the least ability to pass carbon costs through their value chain. A US-only shale E&P firm fits this profile exactly: it cannot diversify geographically, has no refining or retail margin buffer, and its entire asset base consists of fossil fuel reserves that are directly threatened by decarbonisation commitments. Large integrated majors (ExxonMobil, Chevron) escaped significant market reaction precisely because their diversified business models offered natural hedges against any single regulatory shock.

Why are the banks positive (though insignificant)? The small positive CARs for JPMorgan, Citigroup, Bank of America, Wells Fargo, and Goldman Sachs are not statistically significant, but their direction is consistent with the finding by Alessi, Battiston & Kvedaras (2024) — "Over with carbon? Investors' reaction to the Paris Agreement and the US withdrawal", Journal of Financial Stability, Vol. 71 — that regulated financial institutions reduced exposure to high-carbon equities after Paris, while simultaneously positioning themselves for green finance growth (green bonds, sustainability-linked loans, transition financing). The offsetting effects of these two forces produced near-zero net abnormal returns around the announcement date. Ramelli, Wagner, Zeckhauser & Ziegler (2021) — "Investor Rewards to Climate Responsibility", Review of Corporate Finance Studies, Vol. 10, pp. 748–787 — further show that long-horizon investors systematically favoured climate-responsible firms around major climate policy events: banks with stronger ESG commitments may actually have benefitted marginally, consistent with the slightly positive point estimates we observe.

Key takeaways from this event study:

  1. Carbon risk is priced gradually, not in one jump: The literature (Bolton & Kacperczyk 2021, 2023) shows that climate risk embeds itself in expected returns over time, not in sharp announcement-day drops. Widely anticipated agreements like Paris produce muted short-window reactions.
  2. Business model specificity drives abnormal returns: Devon Energy's −18% CAR versus near-zero reactions for ExxonMobil and Chevron illustrates that sector membership alone is not enough — what matters is the firm's specific exposure profile: pure-play, US-only, no downstream operations = maximum sensitivity to climate regulation.
  3. Banks face offsetting forces: Green finance opportunities and fossil fuel lending risk roughly cancel out at the announcement date, producing insignificant but slightly positive reactions — consistent with Alessi et al. (2024) and Ramelli et al. (2021).

8. Portfolio Analysis¶

8.1. What is a portfolio?¶

A portfolio is a collection of assets (stocks) held together. Instead of concentrating all your wealth in one stock, you spread it across many. This diversification reduces risk because individual stocks do not all move in the same direction at the same time.

Value-weighted portfolio: Each stock's weight in the portfolio equals its market capitalisation divided by the total market cap of all stocks. This is exactly how the S&P 500 index itself is constructed — larger companies receive more weight.

Key performance metrics:

Metric Formula Interpretation
Mean return $\bar{r}$ Average monthly or annual return
Volatility ($\sigma$) Standard deviation of returns Total risk (both up and down swings)
Sharpe ratio $(\bar{r} - r_f) / \sigma$ Return per unit of total risk ($r_f$ = risk-free rate)
Sortino ratio $(\bar{r} - r_f) / \sigma_{\text{down}}$ Return per unit of downside risk only

The Sortino ratio is often preferred because it penalises only bad volatility (losses), not good volatility (gains).

8.2. Building an S&P 500 value-weighted portfolio¶

The cell below downloads market capitalisation and company names for all S&P 500 constituents, computes value-weighted portfolio weights, and displays the top 30 holdings.

In [91]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import pandas as pd  # pandas is the standard Python library for working with tabular data (DataFrames)
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)

# Open session
rd.open_session()  # rd.open_session() starts an authenticated connection to Refinitiv/LSEG (needed before queries)

# Get constituents sp
rics_sp500=pd.read_csv("sp500_constituents.csv")  # variable assignment: store a value in 'rics_sp500' so we can reuse it later
rics_sp500 = rics_sp500["RIC"].astype(str).tolist()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'rics_sp500' so we can reuse it later
print(f"Fetching market cap for {len(rics_sp500)} S&P 500 constituents…")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# Collect market cap and name
mcap_panel = rd.get_data(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'mcap_panel' so we can reuse it later
    universe=rics_sp500,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'universe' for the function call
    fields=["TR.CompanyMarketCap", "TR.CompanyName"],  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
    use_field_names_in_headers=True,  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'use_field_names_in_headers' for the function call
) 

#close session
rd.close_session() 

# Rename and clean variables
df = ( mcap_panel .rename(columns={"TR.COMPANYMARKETCAP": "market_cap",  # variable assignment: store a value in 'df' so we can reuse it later
                     "TR.COMPANYNAME": "Company"})) 

# drop if market cap = 0
df = df.loc[df["market_cap"] > 0].copy()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'df' so we can reuse it later

# Compute value-weighted portfolio weights
total_mcap   = df["market_cap"].sum()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'total_mcap' so we can reuse it later
df["weight"] = df["market_cap"] / total_mcap 

# Top 30 holdings.
top30 = df.sort_values("weight", ascending=False).head(30).copy()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'top30' so we can reuse it later
top30["weight_pct"] = 100 * top30["weight"] 

print("Top 30 stocks in the S&P 500 value-weighted portfolio:\n")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print(  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
    top30[["Company", "weight_pct"]] 
    .rename(columns={"weight_pct": "Weight (%)"}) 
    .to_string(index=False, formatters={"Weight (%)": lambda x: f"{x:5.2f}"}) 
) 
print(f"\nCumulative weight of top 30: {top30['weight'].sum():.2%}")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

Fetching market cap for 503 S&P 500 constituents…

The cell below visualises the portfolio with a horizontal bar chart (top 20) and a pie chart (top 10 + "Other").

In [92]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import pandas as pd  # pandas is the standard Python library for working with tabular data (DataFrames)
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt  # matplotlib is a plotting library for charts and figures


fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 6)) 

# ── Bar chart: top 20 ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
top20 = df.sort_values("weight", ascending=False).head(20).copy()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'top20' so we can reuse it later
top20["weight_pct"] = top20["weight"] * 100 

axes[0].barh(top20["Company"].iloc[::-1], top20["weight_pct"].iloc[::-1], 
             color="#4C72B0", edgecolor="white")
axes[0].set_xlabel("Weight (%)") 
axes[0].set_title("Top 20 Holdings (Value-Weighted)", fontweight="bold") 
axes[0].spines[["top", "right"]].set_visible(False) 

# ── Pie chart: top 10 + rest ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
top10     = df.sort_values("weight", ascending=False).head(10).copy()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'top10' so we can reuse it later
top10_pct = top10.set_index("Company")["weight"] * 100  # variable assignment: store a value in 'top10_pct' so we can reuse it later
rest      = pd.Series({"Other": (df["weight"].sum() - top10["weight"].sum()) * 100})  # variable assignment: store a value in 'rest' so we can reuse it later
pie_data  = pd.concat([top10_pct, rest])  # variable assignment: store a value in 'pie_data' so we can reuse it later

axes[1].pie(pie_data.values, labels=pie_data.index, autopct="%1.1f%%", 
            startangle=140, textprops={"fontsize": 8})  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'startangle' for the function call
axes[1].set_title("Portfolio Composition", fontweight="bold") 

plt.suptitle("S&P 500 Value-Weighted Portfolio", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold") 
plt.tight_layout() 
plt.show()

8.3. Portfolio performance — monthly returns, Sharpe and Sortino ratios¶

With the value weights from 8.2 in hand, we now compute the time-series of monthly portfolio returns and summarise its risk-adjusted performance over a 10-year window (2015–2024).

Step 1 — Portfolio return. Each month's portfolio return is the weighted average of the individual stock returns:

$$r_{p,t} = \sum_i w_i \cdot r_{i,t}$$

where $w_i$ is stock $i$'s value weight (market cap share) and $r_{i,t}$ its total return in month $t$. We use fixed weights from 8.2 — a simplification, since a true value-weighted index rebalances every month as market caps change.

Step 2 — Performance metrics. We compute four summary statistics:

Metric Formula Note
Annualised return $(1 + \bar{r}_m)^{12} - 1$ Compounded monthly mean
Annualised volatility $\sigma_m \times \sqrt{12}$ Total risk
Sharpe ratio $\dfrac{\bar{r}_m - r_f}{\sigma_m} \times \sqrt{12}$ Return per unit of total risk
Sortino ratio $\dfrac{\bar{r}_m - r_f}{\sigma_\text{down}} \times \sqrt{12}$ Return per unit of downside risk only

We set $r_f = 0$ for simplicity. The Sortino ratio uses only months where the portfolio return was negative to compute $\sigma_\text{down}$ — it does not penalise upside volatility.

In [93]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import refinitiv.data as rd
import os

BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()

# ── 1. Reload portfolio weights from 8.2 ─────────────────────────
# We need: Instrument (RIC) → weight. These were computed in 8.2 as 'df'.
# If 8.2 has already run in this session, 'df' is available.
# If not, reload constituents + market cap and recompute.
try:
    weights_series = df.set_index("Instrument")["weight"]
    print(f"Loaded {len(weights_series)} weights from session (8.2 already run).")
except NameError:
    sp500_constituents = pd.read_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\sp500_constituents_trbc.csv")
    rics_sp500 = sp500_constituents["TR.RIC"].astype(str).tolist()
    rd.open_session()
    mcap_panel = rd.get_data(
        universe=rics_sp500,
        fields=["TR.CompanyMarketCap"],
        use_field_names_in_headers=True,
    )
    rd.close_session()
    df_tmp = mcap_panel.rename(columns={"TR.COMPANYMARKETCAP": "market_cap"})
    df_tmp = df_tmp.loc[df_tmp["market_cap"] > 0].copy()
    df_tmp["weight"] = df_tmp["market_cap"] / df_tmp["market_cap"].sum()
    weights_series = df_tmp.set_index("Instrument")["weight"]
    print(f"Recomputed {len(weights_series)} weights.")

# ── 2. Download monthly total returns (2015-2024) ─────────────────
DATA_START = "2015-01-01"
DATA_END   = "2024-12-31"

print("Downloading monthly returns — this may take a minute…")
rd.open_session()
ret_raw = rd.get_data(
    universe=weights_series.index.tolist(),
    fields=["TR.TOTALRETURN", "TR.TOTALRETURN.date"],
    parameters={"SDate": DATA_START, "EDate": DATA_END, "Frq": "M"},
    use_field_names_in_headers=True,
)
rd.close_session()

# ── 3. Clean and pivot ────────────────────────────────────────────
ret_raw["Date"] = pd.to_datetime(ret_raw["TR.TOTALRETURN.DATE"])
ret_raw["ret"]  = pd.to_numeric(ret_raw["TR.TOTALRETURN"], errors="coerce") / 100

ret_wide = (
    ret_raw.drop_duplicates(subset=["Date", "Instrument"])
    .pivot(index="Date", columns="Instrument", values="ret")
    .sort_index()
    .astype(float)
)

print(f"Return matrix: {ret_wide.shape[0]} months x {ret_wide.shape[1]} stocks")
print(ret_wide.head())

# Save
ret_wide.to_csv(BASE_FOLDER + "\\sp500_monthly_returns_2015_2024.csv")
print("Saved: sp500_monthly_returns_2015_2024.csv")

Loaded 503 weights from session (8.2 already run).
Downloading monthly returns — this may take a minute…

In [99]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import os

BASE_FOLDER = os.getcwd()

# ── 1. Align weights with return columns ──────────────────────────
# Reindex weights to the stocks for which we have returns;
# fill missing with 0 and renormalise so weights still sum to 1.
w = weights_series.reindex(ret_wide.columns).fillna(0)
w = w / w.sum()

# ── 2. Value-weighted portfolio monthly return ────────────────────
# r_p,t = sum_i( w_i * r_i,t )
# Stocks with missing return in month t contribute 0 (as if held at zero weight).
port_ret = ret_wide.fillna(0).dot(w)
port_ret.name = "S&P 500 VW Portfolio"
port_ret = port_ret[port_ret.index.notna()]  # drop any rows with NaT dates

# ── 3. Performance metrics ────────────────────────────────────────
MONTHS_PER_YEAR = 12
RF_MONTHLY      = 0.0  # risk-free rate set to 0 for simplicity

mean_m   = port_ret.mean()
std_m    = port_ret.std()
mean_ann = (1 + mean_m) ** MONTHS_PER_YEAR - 1  # compounded annualised return
std_ann  = std_m * np.sqrt(MONTHS_PER_YEAR)     # annualised volatility

excess_ret   = port_ret - RF_MONTHLY
sharpe       = excess_ret.mean() / excess_ret.std() * np.sqrt(MONTHS_PER_YEAR)

downside_ret = port_ret[port_ret < RF_MONTHLY]
sortino      = (mean_m - RF_MONTHLY) / downside_ret.std() * np.sqrt(MONTHS_PER_YEAR)

print("=" * 50)
print("S&P 500 Value-Weighted Portfolio — Performance")
print("=" * 50)
print(f"Period                 : {port_ret.index[0].strftime('%Y-%m')} → {port_ret.index[-1].strftime('%Y-%m')}")
print(f"Number of months       : {len(port_ret)}")
print(f"Mean monthly return    : {mean_m*100:+.3f}%")
print(f"Annualised return      : {mean_ann*100:+.2f}%")
print(f"Annualised volatility  : {std_ann*100:.2f}%")
print(f"Sharpe ratio           : {sharpe:.3f}  (Assuming Rf = 0)")
print(f"Sortino ratio          : {sortino:.3f}  (Assuming Rf = 0)")

# ── 4. Plot ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
cumret = (1 + port_ret).cumprod()

fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))

# Panel 1 — cumulative growth of $1
ax1.plot(cumret.index, cumret.values, color="#4C72B0", linewidth=1.8)
ax1.axhline(1, color="black", linewidth=0.8, linestyle="--")
ax1.set_title("Cumulative Growth of \$1 Invested\n(S&P 500 Value-Weighted, 2015\u20132024)", fontweight="bold")
ax1.set_xlabel("Date")
ax1.set_ylabel("Portfolio value (USD)")

# Panel 2 — monthly return distribution
ax2.hist(port_ret * 100, bins=30, color="#4C72B0", edgecolor="white", alpha=0.85)
ax2.axvline(0, color="black", linewidth=0.8, linestyle="--")
ax2.axvline(mean_m * 100, color="red", linewidth=1.2, linestyle="-", label=f"Mean = {mean_m*100:.2f}%")
ax2.set_title("Distribution of Monthly Returns\n(S&P 500 Value-Weighted, 2015–2024)", fontweight="bold")
ax2.set_xlabel("Monthly return (%)")
ax2.set_ylabel("Number of months")
ax2.legend()

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

==================================================
S&P 500 Value-Weighted Portfolio — Performance
==================================================
Period                 : 2015-01 → 2024-12
Number of months       : 120
Mean monthly return    : +1.977%
Annualised return      : +26.49%
Annualised volatility  : 17.06%
Sharpe ratio           : 1.391  (Assuming Rf = 0)
Sortino ratio          : 2.076  (Assuming Rf = 0)
